chapters.title: Chapter 1: Welcome to Nexus 9
chapters.content:
*Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* is a cosmic-opera tabletop roleplaying game set aboard a massive, politically volatile space station at the crossroads of four rival civilizations. Players take on the roles of diplomats, soldiers, spies, smugglers, and mystics navigating a fragile peace that is slowly unraveling under the weight of old grudges, new ambitions, and the stirring of an ancient, malevolent force that has not been seen in a thousand years.

The game is compatible with the Daggerheart system, preserving its story-forward play, emotionally charged consequences, and streamlined mechanics while adding new subsystems for starship combat, diplomatic intrigue, telepathic powers, and galactic-scale mystery. It is designed for campaigns that escalate from local station politics to a galaxy-spanning war, rewarding players who invest in relationships, make hard choices, and accept that every victory comes at a cost.

The tone is hopeful but pressured. This is not grimdark nihilism — it is a universe where people fight for something better, even when the odds are impossible. It is a game about the terrible cost of peace and the even greater cost of war.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 2: History of the Known Galaxy

sections.title: The Primordial Era and the Elders
sections.content:
Billions of years before the current age, the galaxy was silent. Stars burned and died without witness. Then, on a world whose name has been lost to entropy itself, the first spark of sapience ignited within a species now known only as the **Progenitors**. They were naturally immortal, their biology resisting the slow erosion of time. The first among them to achieve true self-awareness was an individual known in later legends as **Orenthal**, the Firstborn. Orenthal did not conquer; he wandered. Over millions of years, he and his kind explored the galaxy and discovered that they were not alone. Other species were beginning to stir — primitive, mortal, fragile — but possessed of a vitality that the immortal Progenitors found fascinating.

Orenthal and his kind chose to nurture these infant races, guiding them toward the stars. These species, lifted from their cradle worlds by the Progenitors' patient tutelage, became known collectively as the **Elders**. Over the course of millions of years, the Elders built empires of staggering scope and power. They mastered hyperspace travel, energy manipulation, and technologies that modern science can barely theorize about. But immortality breeds weariness, and gradually, the Progenitors and the vast majority of the Elders chose to leave the known galaxy. They passed beyond the Rim, seeking new frontiers or perhaps a final peace. Orenthal alone chose to remain, withdrawing to a hidden world deep in uncharted space.

sections.title: The Great Schism
sections.content:
When the great migration occurred, a handful of Elder races chose to stay behind. They believed it was their sacred duty to act as guardians for the next generation of life — the Younger Races that were just beginning to evolve. The two most powerful of these remaining Elders were the **Luminaries** and the **Eclipse**.

Initially, these two ancient powers agreed to a pact of mutual respect. Both would guide the Younger Races, each according to their own philosophy. But their philosophies were diametrically opposed. The Luminaries believed in the absolute necessity of order, structure, and obedience. They viewed the universe as a garden that required constant pruning. The Eclipse held a fundamentally different view: true strength could only be forged in the crucible of conflict. Peace was stagnation; comfort was decay. The Eclipse philosophy dictated that by fostering war, hardship, and competition, the weak would be culled and the strong would ascend to their ultimate potential. The pact shattered, and the Great Schism began.

sections.title: The Cyclical Wars
sections.content:
The war between the Luminaries and the Eclipse was unlike any conflict the galaxy had ever seen. Bound by an ancient, unspoken rule, they rarely struck at each other directly. Instead, they used the Younger Races as instruments. The cycle was brutal and predictable: the Eclipse would awaken from long periods of dormancy, reaching out to the ambitious among the Younger Races, offering forbidden technology and igniting conflicts that spread like wildfire. In response, the Luminaries would rally the remaining species, forming grand alliances of "light" to push back the darkness. These coalitions would eventually drive the Eclipse back to their hidden stronghold world, **The Hollow**, where they would enter a deep hibernation lasting millennia, leaving behind buried caches of their terrifying crystalline warships.

This cycle repeated at least a dozen times over the course of a million years. Each iteration left the galaxy scarred, with entire civilizations reduced to ruins. The Younger Races, caught between two immortal philosophies, were ground to dust and rebuilt, again and again.

sections.title: The Prophecy of Aethelgard
sections.content: A thousand years before the campaign present, the cycle repeated. The Eclipse stirred on their hidden world, and their agents began to move through the galaxy once more. It was during this crisis that a figure of immense significance appeared. **Aethelgard** emerged from a temporal anomaly — a rift in space-time that defied all known science. He bore knowledge of the future and the past, and he spoke with an authority that transcended species and culture. Aethelgard united the fractured Valari castes, founded the **Vanguard** — a covert, multi-species order dedicated to watching for the Eclipse's return — and led a grand coalition that successfully drove the Eclipse back into hibernation. Following the victory, Aethelgard vanished as mysteriously as he had appeared, leaving behind only a cryptic warning: *"The cycle will break, or the cycle will break you."*

sections.title: The Telepath Genesis
sections.content: While the galaxy enjoyed a millennium of relative peace, the Luminaries had not been idle. Recognizing that the Eclipse's crystalline technology was uniquely vulnerable to psychic interference, they initiated a desperate strategy. Approximately two hundred years before the campaign present, Luminary agents secretly visited several Younger Race homeworlds, including Earth. They abducted members of the local population and spliced their DNA with engineered genetic sequences designed to force the evolution of telepathic abilities. Generations later, the first human telepaths began to emerge. The Terran Coalition's government, alarmed and fascinated in equal measure, established the **Mindclave** — an authoritarian organization tasked with identifying, registering, training, and controlling all human telepaths.

sections.title: The Terran-Valari War
sections.content:
The defining event of the modern era was a catastrophic misunderstanding that nearly resulted in the extinction of the human race. The Terran Coalition sent an exploration fleet to the uncharted borders of Valari space. The Valari, already on high alert due to cryptic warnings from the Luminaries, approached with their weapon ports open — a traditional gesture of respect and transparency in their culture. The Terran commander, unfamiliar with Valari customs, panicked and opened fire. The barrage crippled a cruiser and killed the Valari's supreme spiritual leader.

The Valari response was absolute. They declared a holy war against the Terran Coalition and prosecuted it with terrifying efficiency. The war culminated in the **Siege of Sol**, a desperate last stand in orbit around Earth itself. The Terran fleet was shattered, its defenses crumbling. And then, inexplicably, the Valari stopped. At the moment of their total victory, they ceased fire and surrendered unconditionally.

The true reason for this surrender remains one of the galaxy's most closely guarded secrets: during the battle, Valari warriors captured several human pilots for interrogation. When they scanned these prisoners with their sacred soul-reading crystals, they discovered that human souls were intrinsically linked to Valari souls. To destroy humanity would be to destroy a part of themselves. The Grey Assembly ordered an immediate ceasefire, and the truth was sealed behind layers of religious secrecy and political obfuscation.

sections.title: The Nexus Project
sections.content:
In the aftermath of the Terran-Valari War, the galaxy recognized the desperate need for a neutral ground where diplomacy could replace destruction. The Terran Coalition spearheaded the **Nexus Project** — an ambitious plan to construct a massive space station that would serve as a permanent diplomatic hub. The project was plagued by disaster. The first three stations were destroyed during construction by sabotage, design failures, and unexplained accidents. The fourth station, **Nexus 4**, was completed and became operational, only to vanish without a trace mere hours later, swallowed by a temporal anomaly that has never been explained. Stations five through eight were cancelled due to political opposition and funding shortfalls. It was only through sheer political will — and the growing threat of a new Aurelian-Kaelen border war — that the ninth attempt was approved.

**Nexus 9** was completed and became operational one year before the campaign begins. It is humanity's last, best hope for peace. It is also, as the players will discover, a target.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Factions of the Known Galaxy

sections.title: The Terran Coalition
sections.content:
The Terran Coalition is a resilient, adaptive, and politically fractured federal republic. Recovering from the devastating war with the Valari, the Coalition is experiencing a slow, insidious slide into authoritarianism. The government is increasingly opaque and militaristic, influenced heavily by the Mindclave and a cadre of ambitious politicians who believe that security must take precedence over liberty. Civil liberties are being quietly dismantled under the banner of "emergency measures," and a shadow is falling over the Coalition's democratic institutions.

Terran technology is brutalist, utilitarian, and heavily armored, favoring muted greys, olive greens, and matte blacks. Their ships are angular, functional, and built for endurance rather than elegance. The average Terran citizen is pragmatic, hardworking, and increasingly suspicious of alien motives.

| Attribute | Detail |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Government** | Federal republic (sliding toward military junta) |
| **Capital** | Earth (Sol System) |
| **Military Doctrine** | Defense in depth, overwhelming firepower, carrier groups |
| **Primary Resources** | Industrial capacity, population, the Mindclave |
| **Internal Fault Lines** | Democratic ideals vs. military authoritarianism; civilian government vs. Mindclave influence |

sections.title: The Mindclave
sections.content:
The Mindclave is a powerful, politically entrenched organization that controls all human telepaths within the Terran Coalition. Their motto is *"The Clave provides, the Clave endures."* They operate with near-impunity, hunting down unregistered telepaths (known as "rogues") and employing specialized enforcement agents known as **Hounds**. The Mindclave views non-telepaths as obsolete and harbors a long-term ambition to establish telepathic supremacy over human governance.

Telepaths within the Mindclave are rated on a scale from P1 (barely perceptible surface empathy) to P12 (capable of rewriting memories and controlling multiple minds simultaneously). The organization is led by a council of **Directors**, each controlling a sector of operations. The current Director of Nexus 9 operations is **Director Thorne**, a ruthless P10 who believes the Mindclave's destiny is to rule openly.

| Attribute | Detail |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Structure** | Paramilitary hierarchy with Directors, Handlers, and Hounds |
| **Motto** | "The Clave provides, the Clave endures" |
| **Primary Resources** | Monopoly on telepaths, deep political influence, intelligence networks |
| **Internal Fault Lines** | Loyalists vs. rogue telepaths; Directors competing for power; ethical dissenters |

sections.title: The Valari
sections.content:
The Valari are a technologically superior, deeply spiritual species organized into three great castes: the **Vael'Kari** (Warrior Caste), the **Vael'Sari** (Religious Caste), and the **Vael'Tari** (Worker Caste). They are governed by the **Grey Assembly**, a tricameral council where each caste holds equal representation. The Valari are currently fractured by the secret of their surrender at the Siege of Sol, with the Warrior and Religious castes increasingly at odds over the interpretation of ancient prophecies and the proper relationship with humanity.

Valari biology features prominent cranial ridges of crystalline bone, pale skin with a faint luminescent quality, and an extreme intolerance for alcohol (which acts as a potent neurotoxin to their species). Their aesthetic is elegant, sweeping, and organic, favoring stark whites, deep blues, and silver. Their ships resemble cathedral spires in flight.

| Attribute | Detail |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Government** | Tricameral Grey Assembly (Warrior, Religious, Worker castes) |
| **Capital** | Val'Shara (The Veil) |
| **Military Doctrine** | Precision strikes, energy weapons, spiritual warfare |
| **Primary Resources** | Advanced energy weapons, ancient knowledge, soul-crystal technology |
| **Internal Fault Lines** | The surrender secret; Warrior vs. Religious caste schism; isolationists vs. integrationists |

sections.title: The Aurelian Empire
sections.content:
The Aurelian Empire is a declining, decadent hereditary aristocratic monarchy. Once the undisputed center of galactic commerce, the Empire is now characterized by decaying infrastructure, opulent core worlds, and rebellious outer colonies. The Aurelians are master diplomats and intriguers, clinging to past glory with a desperate, performative grandeur. Desperate to reclaim their dominance, elements within the Imperial Court are seeking dark alliances with mysterious benefactors — alliances that will have catastrophic consequences.

Aurelians possess a hereditary **temporal echo** trait, granting them flashes of precognition — brief, unreliable glimpses of possible futures. This ability is both a gift and a curse, as it breeds fatalism and a belief that destiny is inescapable. Their biology features bioluminescent crests along their temples and elongated, expressive fingers. Their aesthetic is opulent and ornate, favoring rich purples, golds, and crimson.

| Attribute | Detail |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Government** | Hereditary monarchy with a Noble Court (the Imperial Conclave) |
| **Capital** | Aurelia Prime (The Gilded Reach) |
| **Military Doctrine** | Overwhelming capital-ship firepower, noble officer corps, expendable conscripts |
| **Primary Resources** | Wealth, diplomatic leverage, precognitive seers, Eclipse-enhanced technology |
| **Internal Fault Lines** | Decadent nobility vs. pragmatic military; the corrupting influence of the Eclipse; colonial rebellions |

sections.title: The Kaelen Syndicate
sections.content:
The Kaelen Syndicate is a fierce, aggressive revolutionary council and military junta. Recently liberated from centuries of brutal Aurelian occupation, the Kaelen are rapidly militarizing, driven by a desire for justice that frequently bleeds into a hunger for revenge. They are bound by the **Ven'Kari**, a sacred blood oath sworn against their enemies — an oath that can only be fulfilled by the complete destruction of the target or the death of the oath-swearer.

Kaelen biology features dense, scaled skin in hues of rust and ochre, redundant organ systems (including two hearts and a secondary liver), and powerful musculature adapted for survival on their environmentally devastated homeworlds. Their aesthetic is industrial, aggressive, and scarred, favoring rust reds, burnt oranges, and dark browns. Their ships are ugly, functional, and bristling with weapons.

| Attribute | Detail |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Government** | Revolutionary Council (military junta) |
| **Capital** | Kaelen Prime (The Scorch) |
| **Military Doctrine** | Aggressive assault, boarding actions, guerrilla warfare |
| **Primary Resources** | Battle-hardened veterans, raw materials, sheer determination |
| **Internal Fault Lines** | Revenge vs. stability; radical militarists vs. moderate reformers; the Ven'Kari's self-destructive nature |

sections.title: The Luminaries
sections.content:
The Luminaries are ancient, enigmatic beings representing the philosophy of order and obedience. They view the universe as a chaotic system that must be ordered to survive, and they have spent millions of years manipulating the Younger Races toward conformity and structure. They interact with lesser species through projected **hard-light avatars** — beings of pure, blinding radiance that inspire awe and terror in equal measure. Their true forms are unknown; no living member of the Younger Races has ever seen a Luminary outside its projection.

The Luminary ambassador to Nexus 9 is known only as **The Emissary**. It communicates in cryptic pronouncements and seems to possess knowledge of events before they occur. Its motives are inscrutable, but it has made one thing clear: the Eclipse are returning, and the Younger Races must prepare — or be consumed.

sections.title: The Eclipse
sections.content:
The Eclipse are ancient, malevolent beings representing the philosophy of chaos and evolution through conflict. They believe that true strength is forged only in the crucible of war, and they have spent millions of years whispering to the ambitious, igniting conflicts that consume entire civilizations. The Eclipse manifest as **geometric, crystalline void-shapes** — angular, impossible forms that seem to absorb light rather than reflect it. Their ships are grown from crystalline matrices, and they feed on the psychic energy of their victims.

The Eclipse operate through agents among the Younger Races. Their primary agent on Nexus 9 is a human known only as **The Whisperer** — a charming, well-dressed figure who approaches the powerful with a single, devastating question: *"What would you become?"*

sections.title: The Vanguard
sections.content:
The Vanguard is a covert, multi-species order founded a thousand years ago by the temporal prophet Aethelgard. Their mission is singular: watch for the Eclipse's return and prepare the galaxy to resist. The Vanguard operates in small cells, hidden among the general population, communicating through encrypted channels and dead drops. They are underfunded, understaffed, and increasingly desperate, as the Eclipse have begun systematically hunting them down.

The Vanguard's symbol is the **Star-Sword** — a blade piercing a stylized star. Their ships are fast, stealthy, and lightly armed, designed for reconnaissance rather than combat. The senior Vanguard operative on Nexus 9 is **Lyra**, a young, intense woman who carries the weight of her order's dwindling numbers on her shoulders.

sections.title: The Umbral Covenant
sections.content: The Umbral Covenant is a shadowy network of species and individuals who serve the Eclipse, either willingly or through coercion. Their most feared agents are the **Keepers** — beings implanted with a parasitic crystalline organism that overrides their free will and binds them to the Eclipse's hive-mind. Keepers appear normal until activated, at which point their eyes turn solid black and they become relentless, single-minded instruments of destruction.

sections.title: The Technoseers
sections.content: The Technoseers are a secretive order of bio-technological adepts who have merged their bodies with advanced cybernetic implants derived from ancient Elder technology. They are wanderers, scholars, and occasional mercenaries, feared and distrusted by most civilizations. The origin of their technology is a closely guarded secret: it was reverse-engineered from Eclipse crystalline matrices, making the Technoseers both invaluable allies and potential liabilities.

sections.title: The League of Unaligned Worlds
sections.content:
The League is a loose diplomatic confederation of dozens of smaller species with varying levels of technological development. They hold a collective seat on the Nexus 9 Advisory Council but are frequently divided by internal rivalries. Notable member species include the **Drazi** (a fiercely territorial avian species governed by a ritualized combat system), the **Brakiri** (a mercantile species obsessed with astrology and commerce), and the **Gaim** (an insectoid hive-mind that communicates through a single appointed Speaker).

---

chapters.title: Chapter 4: Culture, Daily Life, and the Station

sections.title: The Galactic Map
sections.content:
The known galaxy is situated within the Orion Arm, connected by a network of ancient, artificially constructed hyperspace gates left behind by the Elders. Travel without a gate is possible but exponentially slower and more dangerous, requiring massive onboard jump engines that leave the ship vulnerable for several minutes after each transition.

| Region | Controller | Character |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **The Core Worlds** | Terran Coalition | Dense, industrialized, heavily policed, resource-hungry |
| **The Veil** | Valari | Nebula-shrouded, difficult navigation, sacred space |
| **The Gilded Reach** | Aurelian Empire | Decaying grandeur, opulent core worlds, rebellious colonies |
| **The Scorch** | Kaelen Syndicate | Resource-rich, environmentally devastated, heavily fortified |
| **The Coriana Sector** | Neutral Zone | Strategic crossroads where all borders meet; location of Nexus 9 |
| **The Rimward Marches** | Unclaimed | Lawless, uncharted, home to pirates, explorers, and ancient secrets |

sections.title: Nexus 9: The Station
sections.content:
Nexus 9 is a massive, five-ringed torus approximately eight kilometers in diameter, with five rotating habitat rings connected to a central hub by reinforced transit spokes. The central hub houses the station's **Command Center**, primary communications array, and the **Nexus Core** — a mysterious energy source of unknown origin discovered during construction. The Core provides the station with far more power than its conventional fusion reactors, but its true nature and purpose remain a closely guarded secret.

The station is commanded by **Commander Elias Vance**, a decorated Terran Coalition officer who fought in the Siege of Sol and carries the scars — both physical and psychological — of that conflict. Vance is a pragmatist who believes in the station's mission but is increasingly frustrated by the political interference of his own government.

| Ring | Name | Function | Sensory Character |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Blue Ring** | Diplomatic Quarter | Embassies, the Grand Assembly Hall, VIP residences | Ozone and expensive incense; cool, artificial lighting; eerily quiet corridors |
| **Green Ring** | Commercial District | The Promenade, shops, restaurants, entertainment | Exotic spices, roasting meats, engine coolant; neon signs in a dozen languages; chaotic crowds |
| **Red Ring** | Military/Security | Docking bays, the Brig, Coalition Security HQ | Gun oil and antiseptic; harsh lighting; slightly heavier gravity (1.1g) |
| **Gold Ring** | Scientific | Xenobiology labs, medical facilities, research centers | Sterile scrubbers and chemical preservatives; bright, clinical lighting |
| **Grey Ring** | Industrial/Residential | Worker housing, refugee camps, the Warrens, the Underbelly | Sweat, rust, recycled water; erratic lighting; inconsistent gravity |

| Key Location | Ring | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| The Grand Assembly Hall | Blue | The main diplomatic chamber where faction ambassadors convene |
| The Promenade | Green | The station's commercial heart — shops, restaurants, entertainment |
| The Underbelly | Green (Lower) | The black market district beneath the Promenade |
| The Brig | Red | The station's detention facility, run by Coalition Security |
| Docking Bay Alpha | Red | The primary military docking facility for capital ships |
| The Xenobiology Lab | Gold | A state-of-the-art research facility for studying alien life |
| The Warrens | Grey | Worker housing and refugee camps in the industrial ring |
| The Nexus Core | Central Hub | The mysterious power source at the station's heart |
| The Zero-G Spine | Central Hub | A weightless corridor running the length of the central hub |
| Grey Level 17 | Grey (Deep) | An unfinished, sealed-off section; rumors of strange phenomena persist |

sections.title: Currency and Commerce
sections.content: The standard currency on Nexus 9 is the **Credit**, a digital fiat currency backed by the Terran Coalition but accepted universally. Physical currency is rare, usually taking the form of untraceable "chit-sticks" used in the Underbelly. Barter is common in the Green and Grey rings. Information, favors, and rare Earth commodities (like real coffee or un-recycled water) are often more valuable than Credits.

sections.title: Lexicon and Station Slang
sections.content:
| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Spinward / Retrograde** | Standard directions on the station, referring to the rings' rotation |
| **Spacing** | Execution by airlock; also a general curse ("Space that idea") |
| **Chit** | A unit of currency, or a physical data-stick containing currency |
| **The Hum** | The omnipresent background noise of life support; "listening to the hum" means trying to sleep |
| **Dust** | Contraband or illegal narcotics |
| **Suit** | Derogatory term for a diplomat or corporate executive |
| **Rimrunner** | A smuggler or explorer who operates beyond the gate network |
| **Clave-touched** | Derogatory term for someone suspected of telepathic influence |

---

chapters.title: Chapter 5: Technology Principles
chapters.content:
The technology of *Nexus 9* is grounded in a "used future" aesthetic. It is functional, often mass-produced, and frequently repaired with scavenged parts. While the Younger Races have achieved faster-than-light travel and directed-energy weapons, they are still children compared to the ancient powers.

**Hyperspace Travel.** Faster-than-light travel is achieved by entering hyperspace, a parallel dimension where distances are compressed. The primary method of entry is through the **Gate Network**, a series of massive, ancient structures left behind by the Elders. Activating a gate requires a precise navigational sequence and a significant energy expenditure. Ships without access to a gate must use onboard **Jump Engines**, which are massive, expensive, and require a tremendous amount of power, often leaving the ship vulnerable for several minutes after a jump.

**Communications.** Faster-than-light communication is possible via **Tachyon Relays**, but it is expensive and bandwidth-limited. Most long-distance communication relies on courier ships carrying physical data-drives. Within a star system, standard radio and laser communications are used, subject to light-speed delays.

**Artificial Intelligence.** True, self-aware AI is strictly forbidden by the Terran Coalition and the Valari, following a disastrous cybernetic revolt centuries ago. Most "smart" systems are highly advanced expert systems or **Virtual Intelligences (VIs)**, capable of complex tasks but lacking true sapience or creativity.

**Weaponry.** The standard infantry weapon is the **Plasma Pistol** or **Carbine**, which fires a bolt of superheated ionized gas. Kinetic weapons (slug-throwers) are still used, particularly by the Kaelen Syndicate, due to their reliability and armor-penetrating capabilities. Ship-to-ship combat relies on massive plasma cannons, railguns, and guided torpedoes.

**Medicine.** Medical technology is highly advanced, capable of regrowing limbs and curing most diseases. However, it is unevenly distributed. The wealthy Aurelian core worlds have access to near-immortality treatments, while the Warrens of Nexus 9 rely on black-market med-kits and scavenged antibiotics.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 6: Metaphysical Principles
chapters.content:
The universe of *Nexus 9* is not purely materialistic. There are forces at work that defy conventional scientific explanation, rooted in the ancient history of the galaxy and the nature of consciousness itself.

**Telepathy.** Telepathy is not magic; it is a forced evolutionary trait seeded into the human genome by the Luminaries. It operates on the principle of **Quantum Entanglement of Consciousness** — a telepath's brain can perceive and manipulate the quantum states of other minds. This requires immense concentration and can cause physical trauma (Stress) if the telepath pushes too far. Telepathy is rated on a P-scale from P1 (faint surface empathy) to P12 (full cognitive override).

**The Luminary Philosophy (Order).** The Luminaries believe that the universe is a chaotic system that must be ordered to survive. Their technology is based on **Hard-Light** and **Energy Manipulation**, creating structures that are perfectly geometric and immutable. They view telepathy as a tool for enforcing order and conformity.

**The Eclipse Philosophy (Chaos).** The Eclipse believe that evolution only occurs through conflict and destruction. Their technology is **Organic and Crystalline**, adapting and mutating based on the environment. Their ships are grown, not built, and they feed on the psychic energy of their victims. They view telepathy as a weapon to spread fear and discord.

**Valari Soul-Crystals.** The Valari possess a unique technology that bridges the gap between the physical and the metaphysical. Their **Soul-Crystals** are naturally occurring resonant structures that can record and store the emotional and psychic imprint of a living being. These crystals are used in their religious ceremonies, their justice system, and, most importantly, to determine the truth of a person's intentions. The discovery that human souls resonate with Valari crystals is the foundation of the fragile peace between the two species — and the most dangerous secret in the galaxy.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 7: Faction Pressures and Dynamics

sections.title: Faction Doctrine, Resources, and Fault Lines
sections.content:
| Faction | Core Doctrine | Primary Resources | Internal Fault Lines |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Terran Coalition** | Pragmatism, expansion, centralized control | Industrial capacity, sheer numbers, the Mindclave | Democratic ideals vs. military authoritarianism |
| **The Mindclave** | Telepathic supremacy, absolute loyalty | Monopoly on telepaths, deep political influence | Rogue telepaths; internal power struggles among Directors |
| **The Valari** | Spiritual purity, caste obedience, martial honor | Advanced energy weapons, ancient knowledge | The surrender secret; Warrior vs. Religious caste schism |
| **Aurelian Empire** | Reclaiming lost glory by any means | Wealth, diplomatic leverage, precognitive seers | Decadent nobility vs. pragmatic military; Eclipse corruption |
| **Kaelen Syndicate** | Justice through strength; never be subjugated again | Battle-hardened veterans, raw materials | Revenge vs. stability; radical vs. moderate factions |
| **The Vanguard** | Vigilance, sacrifice, preservation of life | Covert networks, ancient Aethelgard technology | Dwindling numbers; moral cost of operating outside the law |

---

chapters.content:
The galaxy is a powder keg. The fragile peace maintained on Nexus 9 is constantly tested by overlapping pressures that threaten to ignite a new galactic war.

**Diplomatic Pressures.** The Aurelian Empire and the Kaelen Syndicate are locked in a cold war over border colonies. The Terran Coalition is attempting to mediate, but their own internal slide toward authoritarianism makes them an unreliable arbiter. The Valari remain aloof, their diplomatic efforts hampered by the secret of their surrender.

**Commercial Pressures.** The destruction of the previous Nexus stations disrupted major trade routes. Nexus 9 is now the primary economic hub of the sector. Control over the station's docking bays and tariffs is fiercely contested. The Underbelly thrives on this instability, smuggling everything from weapons to Eclipse artifacts.

**Religious Pressures.** The Valari are experiencing a schism between their Warrior and Religious castes over the interpretation of ancient prophecies. Meanwhile, a new cult is rising in the lower levels of Nexus 9, preaching the return of Aethelgard and the imminent end of the cycle.

**Espionage Pressures.** Every faction has spies on Nexus 9. The Mindclave uses telepaths to steal state secrets, while the Vanguard operates in the shadows to uncover Eclipse infiltrators. Information is the most valuable currency on the station.

**War Pressures.** The threat of a new galactic war is ever-present. The Aurelians are secretly rearming with Eclipse technology, the Kaelen are massing troops on their borders, and the Terran Coalition is fortifying the Core Worlds.

chapters.title: Chapter 8: The Campaign Promise
chapters.content:
*Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* is a game of cosmic opera, political intrigue, and ancient mysteries. You play as diplomats, smugglers, soldiers, and telepaths navigating the fragile peace of a massive space station. The universe is hopeful but pressured. Diplomacy is as dangerous as a firefight, and the choices you make will ripple across the galaxy.

This is not a game of simple good versus evil. The factions you interact with have deep histories, legitimate grievances, and conflicting visions for the future. You will be forced to make hard choices, forge unlikely alliances, and decide what you are willing to sacrifice to prevent the galaxy from burning.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 9: Core Mechanics: The Hope/Fear System
chapters.content:
The game uses the Daggerheart Hope/Fear system to resolve actions where the outcome is uncertain and the consequences matter.

When you attempt a risky action, you roll **2d12** (one designated as the Hope die, the other as the Fear die) and add your relevant Trait modifier.

*   **Success:** If the total equals or exceeds the GM's Difficulty, you succeed. You achieve your goal without major complications.
*   **Failure:** If the total is less than the Difficulty, you fail. The GM introduces a complication, a new threat, or a consequence that pushes the story forward.
*   **With Hope:** If the Hope die rolls higher than the Fear die, you gain 1 Hope (or clear 1 Stress). Hope can be spent to activate special abilities, help allies, or push yourself beyond your normal limits.
*   **With Fear:** If the Fear die rolls higher than the Hope die, the GM gains 1 Fear (or you mark 1 Stress). The GM can spend Fear to activate adversary abilities, introduce new hazards, or advance Threat Clocks.
*   **Critical Success:** If both dice show the same number, you succeed with Hope, and the effect is maximized. You achieve your goal perfectly, often gaining an unexpected benefit.

---

chapters.title: Chapter 10: Character Creation

sections.title: The Six Traits
sections.content:
Your character's capabilities are defined by six Traits, which range from -1 to +3 at character creation.

1.  **Agility:** Speed, reflexes, and physical coordination. Used for dodging, piloting, and zero-G maneuvering.
2.  **Strength:** Physical power, endurance, and raw force. Used for lifting, breaking things, and resisting physical trauma.
3.  **Finesse:** Precision, manual dexterity, and sleight of hand. Used for picking locks, repairing delicate machinery, and shooting firearms.
4.  **Instinct:** Perception, intuition, and situational awareness. Used for spotting ambushes, reading body language, and telepathic defense.
5.  **Presence:** Charisma, force of personality, and leadership. Used for negotiation, intimidation, and rallying allies.
6.  **Knowledge:** Education, technical expertise, and memory. Used for hacking, xenobiology, and recalling historical facts.

sections.title: Ancestries
sections.content:
Your Ancestry represents your species and provides a unique mechanical benefit reflecting your biology or cultural upbringing.

*   **Human:** Adaptable, resilient, and ubiquitous. Humans are the driving force behind the Terran Coalition and the Nexus Project. They are known for their stubbornness and their ability to bounce back from disaster.
    *   *Feature:* **Resilient Spirit.** You gain Advantage on all rolls to recover from Stress or heal HP during a short rest.
*   **Valari:** Spiritual, disciplined, and physically imposing. The Valari are divided into three rigid castes and are bound by ancient traditions. They possess a deep connection to the metaphysical fabric of the universe.
    *   *Feature:* **Ancestral Memory.** You gain Advantage on rolls related to history, religion, or melee combat.
*   **Aurelian:** Aristocratic, decadent, and precognitive. The Aurelians are the remnants of a once-great empire. They possess a hereditary "temporal echo" trait that allows them to glimpse possible futures.
    *   *Feature:* **Temporal Echo.** You can spend 1 Hope to ask the GM one question about the immediate future (e.g., "What will happen if I open this door?"). The GM must answer truthfully, though the answer may be cryptic.
*   **Kaelen:** Tough, aggressive, and fiercely loyal. The Kaelen evolved on a harsh, resource-depleted world and survived centuries of Aurelian occupation. Their biology features dense, scaled skin and redundant organ systems.
    *   *Feature:* **Hardened Physiology.** You gain +1 to your maximum HP, and you can ignore the effects of the first environmental hazard you encounter each session.

sections.title: Communities
sections.content:
Your Community represents your background, your upbringing, and the faction that shaped your worldview.

*   **Terran Coalition:** You grew up in the industrialized Core Worlds or served in the Coalition military. You understand bureaucracy, chain of command, and mass-produced technology.
    *   *Feature:* **System Access.** You gain Advantage on rolls related to operating standard technology, navigating bureaucracy, or calling in military favors.
*   **The Mindclave:** You were raised within the telepathic order, either as a loyal Hound or a reluctant initiate. You understand the subtle cues of mental manipulation.
    *   *Feature:* **Psychic Sensitivity.** You can sense the presence of other telepaths or strong emotional residue within 60 feet, even through solid walls.
*   **The Underbelly:** You survived the station's black market, the Grey Ring warrens, or the lawless Rim. You know how to stay hidden and where to find things that don't officially exist.
    *   *Feature:* **Street Smarts.** You gain Advantage on rolls to find illegal goods, locate hidden paths, or negotiate with criminals.
*   **The Vanguard:** You were trained by the covert order founded by Aethelgard. You understand the ancient history of the galaxy and the true nature of the Eclipse threat.
    *   *Feature:* **Shadow Operative.** You gain Advantage on stealth rolls in dim light or darkness, and you can identify Eclipse artifacts on sight.

---



> *"A crew is not just a collection of people on a ship. It is a shared debt, a shared secret, and a shared target on your backs. Before you fly, you must know who you are flying with."*

---

chapters.content: Creating a character in *Nexus 9* is a collaborative process that roots your character deeply in the setting's lore and politics. It involves choosing your Ancestry (species), Community (faction background), and Class. You then assign values to your six Traits.

chapters.title: 1. The Session Zero Framework

sections.title: 1.1 Safety Tools
sections.content:
The universe of *Nexus 9* is dangerous, but the gaming table must be safe. The GM should implement the following tools:

*   **Lines and Veils:** Before character creation, every player (including the GM) lists their Lines (topics that will absolutely not appear in the game) and Veils (topics that may exist in the world but will only be described in a "fade to black" manner). Common Veils in *Nexus 9* might include torture, explicit gore, or harm to children.
*   **The X-Card:** A physical card (or digital equivalent) placed in the center of the table. If any player touches or invokes the X-Card, the current scene immediately stops and the narrative is edited to remove the offending content, no questions asked.
*   **Open Door Policy:** Any player can leave the table at any time for any reason without judgment.

sections.title: 1.2 Tone Calibration
sections.content:
*Nexus 9* can be played in several different ways. The group should discuss and agree on the campaign's primary tone:

| Tone | Description | Primary Focus |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **The Diplomatic Corps** | High-society intrigue, embassy parties, and preventing war through words. | Social conflict, faction reputation, espionage. |
| **The Underbelly** | Smuggling, black markets, gang wars, and surviving the Grey Ring. | Heists, street-level combat, debt management. |
| **The Frontier Patrol** | Exploring derelicts, fighting pirates, and investigating ancient ruins. | Ship combat, exploration, cosmic horror. |
| **The Vanguard** | Hunting Eclipse-Corrupted agents and preparing for the coming war. | Investigation, tactical combat, uncovering secrets. |

sections.title: 1.3 The Campaign Premise
sections.content:
Once the tone is set, the GM should present the core premise of the campaign. If using the official *Gathering Storm* campaign book, the premise is:

> *"You are an independent crew operating out of Nexus 9. You are deeply in debt, politically unaffiliated, and desperate for work. The fragile peace between the four major powers is cracking, and you are about to be caught in the middle."*

---

chapters.content:
*Nexus 9* is a game of political intrigue, moral ambiguity, and cosmic horror. Because the setting deals with themes of war, corruption, prejudice, and sacrifice, establishing a strong foundation of trust and shared expectations before the dice hit the table is critical.

Session Zero is a dedicated meeting where the GM and players collaboratively build the campaign's tone, establish safety tools, and create the crew as a cohesive unit.

chapters.title: 2. Crew Creation

sections.title: Step 1: The Ship
sections.content:
Unless the campaign specifically dictates otherwise, the crew begins with a starship. This ship is their home, their transport, and their largest financial liability.

The group must collectively choose one of the following starting frames:

| Frame | Best For | Starting HP | Starting Evasion |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Hermes-Class Courier** | Diplomacy, speed, running blockades. | 20 | 12 |
| **Orion-Class Freighter** | Smuggling, salvage, heavy cargo. | 30 | 10 |
| **Scarab-Class Gunship** | Bounty hunting, mercenary work, combat. | 40 | 9 |

**Name the Ship:** The crew must agree on a name for their vessel.
**The Quirk:** Every used ship has a quirk. Roll 1d6:
1. The life support smells faintly of ozone and old copper.
2. The comms array occasionally picks up encrypted Mindclave static.
3. The artificial gravity in the cargo bay stutters during hyperspace jumps.
4. The ship's registry is flagged in the Aurelian Empire (expect frequent stops).
5. There is a sealed, welded-shut compartment near the engine core.
6. The ship's AI has a sarcastic, slightly morbid personality.

sections.title: Step 2: The Debt
sections.content:
No one gets a ship for free. The crew begins the game owing a massive debt (typically 500,000 Credits) to a powerful entity. This debt is the primary engine that drives the crew to take dangerous jobs.

Roll 1d6 or choose the Crew's Creditor:

| d6 | The Creditor | The Catch |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1** | **The Kaelen Syndicate** | They expect you to move illegal cargo no questions asked. |
| **2** | **A Terran Megacorporation** | They require you to conduct corporate espionage against rivals. |
| **3** | **An Aurelian Noble** | They demand you transport them (or their illicit goods) discreetly. |
| **4** | **Jax (Underbelly Fixer)** | He takes a 20% cut of every job you do until the debt is paid. |
| **5** | **The Vanguard** | The ship is technically theirs; you are expected to drop everything when they call. |
| **6** | **A Technoseer Magus** | They don't want Credits; they want Progenitor artifacts. |

sections.title: Step 3: Crew Roles
sections.content:
Every ship needs a crew. The players must decide who fills which primary role during starship combat and exploration. A character's Class often dictates their best role, but anyone can attempt any job.

*   **The Helm (Pilot/Operative):** Controls movement, evasion, and pursuit.
*   **Tactical (Marine/Engineer):** Controls weapons, targeting, and suppressive fire.
*   **Engineering (Engineer/Medic):** Manages power routing, emergency repairs, and shields.
*   **Sensors/Comms (Mystic/Envoy):** Manages scanning, electronic warfare, and communications.
*   **Quartermaster (Broker):** Manages cargo, fuel, and bribes (used primarily out of combat).

---

chapters.content: In *Nexus 9*, you do not create individual characters in a vacuum. You create a **Crew**. The Crew has its own resources, its own history, and its own problems.

chapters.title: 3. Character Creation & Relationship Mapping

sections.title: 3.1 The Bonds of the Crew
sections.content:
Every character must establish a **Bond** with at least two other characters in the crew. These bonds represent past experiences, shared trauma, or mutual reliance.

Players take turns choosing a prompt from the table below and assigning it to another player's character. The two players then briefly discuss the details of that shared history.

| d12 | Relationship Prompt |
|:---:|:---|
| **1** | You saved my life during a firefight in the Grey Ring. I owe you, and I hate owing people. |
| **2** | We both know a dangerous secret about the Aurelian Empire. If they find out we know, we're dead. |
| **3** | You caught me stealing from the ship's stores, but you didn't tell the others. Why? |
| **4** | We served on opposite sides of a border skirmish before joining this crew. The tension is still there. |
| **5** | You are the only one who knows about my telepathic sensitivity (or my lack thereof). |
| **6** | We spent a week stranded in an escape pod together. We know each other's deepest fears. |
| **7** | I blame you for the job that went wrong and put us in this massive debt. |
| **8** | You remind me of someone I lost during the Eclipse Awakening. I am overly protective of you. |
| **9** | We share a contact in the Underbelly who only trusts the two of us. |
| **10** | I suspect you are secretly working for the Vanguard, but I haven't confronted you yet. |
| **11** | We both lost our homes to the Kaelen Syndicate. We share a burning desire for revenge. |
| **12** | You are the only reason I haven't abandoned this crew and this station. |

sections.title: 3.2 The Secret
sections.content:
In addition to Bonds, every character must have one **Secret**. This is something they are actively hiding from the rest of the crew. The GM should know this secret and use it to complicate the narrative later in the campaign.

Players can invent their own secret or roll on the table below:

| d8 | The Secret |
|:---:|:---|
| **1** | I am a sleeper agent for the Mindclave, reporting on the crew's activities. |
| **2** | I am not who I say I am; I stole this identity to escape a massive bounty. |
| **3** | I am infected with a dormant strain of Eclipse corruption. I can hear them whispering. |
| **4** | I know the location of a pristine Progenitor artifact, but I need the crew's help to get it. |
| **5** | I am secretly a noble of the Aurelian Empire, hiding from assassins. |
| **6** | I murdered the previous owner of our ship. |
| **7** | I am addicted to a rare, highly illegal combat stim, and my supply is running low. |
| **8** | I have a family hidden in the Grey Ring, and all my share of the Credits goes to keeping them safe. |

chapters.content:
Once the Crew's foundation (the ship, the debt, and the roles) is established, players create their individual characters using the standard *Nexus 9* rules (Ancestry, Community, Class, and Subclass).

However, a crew that has been flying together already has history. To simulate this, the group engages in **Relationship Mapping**.

chapters.title: 4. Campaign Integration
chapters.content:
Once the Crew is built, the GM should take a few minutes to integrate their choices into the first session of the campaign.

*   **The Debt Collector:** The first NPC the crew interacts with should be a representative of their Creditor, demanding a payment or offering a dangerous job to clear a portion of the debt.
*   **The Ship's Quirk:** The GM should trigger the ship's quirk during the first high-stress situation (e.g., the gravity stutters just as a firefight breaks out in the cargo bay).
*   **The Bonds:** The GM should present situations that test the established bonds. If two characters share a secret about the Aurelian Empire, the first job should involve an Aurelian noble.

By the end of Session Zero, the players should feel like a cohesive unit with a shared history, a clear motivation (the debt), and a tangible connection to the dangerous universe of *Nexus 9*.

---

*End of Session Zero & Crew Creation Toolkit*

chapters.title: Character Creation
chapters.content:
## Building Your Agent

Creating a character in *Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* follows a structured, step-by-step process. Each step builds on the last, producing a fully realized operative ready to face the dangers of the Known Galaxy. This chapter walks you through every decision, from choosing your ancestry to recording your starting gear.

Before you begin, you will need a character sheet (see Appendix), two twelve-sided dice (2d12), and a set of standard polyhedral dice (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20).

---

## Step 1: Choose Your Ancestry

Your Ancestry represents the species or cultural lineage your character was born into. It determines your physical traits, your starting language, and one innate ability that reflects your heritage.

Nexus 9 features the following Ancestries:

| Ancestry | Description | Ancestry Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Terran** | Humans from the Coalition core worlds. Adaptable, ambitious, and widespread. | *Tenacity:* Once per Long Rest, when you fail an action roll, you may immediately reroll it and take the new result. |
| **Aurelian** | Tall, long-lived humanoids with gilded skin and a culture of imperial grandeur. | *Imperial Bearing:* You gain Advantage on Presence rolls when interacting with anyone who recognizes your noble heritage. |
| **Kaelen** | Stocky, resilient humanoids with mottled grey-green skin, adapted to harsh frontier worlds. | *Born Survivor:* You gain Advantage on Instinct rolls to resist environmental hazards (extreme cold, radiation, low oxygen). |
| **Valari** | Slender, crystalline-boned humanoids with luminous eyes and a deep spiritual tradition. | *Crystal Resonance:* You can sense the presence of Progenitor technology or Eclipse corruption within Close range without a roll. |
| **Mindclave-Born** | Terrans or Aurelians born with latent telepathic potential, identified and raised within the Mindclave. | *Psychic Sensitivity:* You can read the surface emotions (not thoughts) of any creature within Very Close range as a Free Action. |
| **Frontier-Born** | Mixed-heritage individuals from the unaligned border worlds — scrappy, resourceful, and self-reliant. | *Resourceful:* Once per Short Rest, you may produce a common, non-weapon item worth 50 Credits or less that you "happened to have." |
| **Synthetic** | Artificial beings — androids, uploaded consciousnesses, or bio-mechanical constructs. | *Machine Logic:* You are immune to telepathic intrusion and the Disoriented condition. You do not need to eat, drink, or breathe, but you require maintenance during Long Rests. |

---

## Step 2: Choose Your Community

Your Community represents where you grew up and the values that shaped you. It provides one Experience (a narrative skill bonus) and one Community Feature.

| Community | Description | Starting Experience | Community Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Core Worlder** | Raised on a wealthy, well-connected inner world. | Bureaucracy (+2) | *Connections:* You know the name and location of one mid-ranking official in any major faction. |
| **Station Rat** | Grew up on Nexus 9 or a similar orbital habitat. | Street Smarts (+2) | *Station Knowledge:* You always know the fastest route between any two points on a station, and you can identify safe houses and bolt-holes without a roll. |
| **Frontier Settler** | Raised on a remote colony world with little infrastructure. | Survival (+2) | *Self-Reliant:* During a Short Rest, you can repair one piece of damaged equipment without tools or an Engineer. |
| **Military Brat** | Grew up on Coalition military bases and warships. | Tactics (+2) | *Drilled:* In the first round of any combat, you act before any adversary, regardless of the spotlight order. |
| **Underbelly Dweller** | Raised in the criminal underworld of a major station. | Deception (+2) | *Underworld Contacts:* Once per session, you can locate a black-market dealer, fence, or informant in your current location without a roll. |
| **Temple Raised** | Raised in a Valari monastery or Aurelian religious order. | Theology (+2) | *Inner Peace:* Once per Long Rest, you may clear 2 Stress without spending Hope or taking a rest action. |
| **Exile** | You were cast out from your original community — a political dissident, a criminal, or a refugee. | Persuasion (+2) | *Nothing Left to Lose:* When you are at 3 or fewer HP, you gain +1 to all action rolls. |
| **Technoseer Apprentice** | Trained (briefly) by the Technoseers before leaving or being expelled. | Engineering (+2) | *Progenitor Intuition:* You gain Advantage on Knowledge rolls to identify, activate, or repair Progenitor technology. |

---

## Step 3: Choose Your Class

Your Class defines your core combat role, your primary abilities, and the Domain cards available to you. Nexus 9 features eight classes, each adapted from the Daggerheart core framework.

Choose one of the following classes. Full class descriptions, subclass features, and Domain card lists are found in Book Three.

| Class | Role | Domains | Starting HP | Starting Evasion |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Envoy** | Face / Social Specialist | Grace & Codex | 5 | 10 |
| **Operative** | Striker / Infiltrator | Midnight & Grace | 6 | 12 |
| **Pilot** | Skirmisher / Vehicle Specialist | Bone & Sage | 6 | 12 |
| **Marine** | Frontline Combatant | Blade & Bone | 6 | 11 |
| **Engineer** | Protector / Support Tank | Valor & Blade | 7 | 9 |
| **Mystic** | Ranged Damage / Telepathy | Arcana & Midnight | 6 | 10 |
| **Broker** | Utility / Controller | Sage & Arcana | 6 | 10 |
| **Medic** | Healer / Support | Splendor & Valor | 7 | 9 |

---

## Step 4: Choose Your Subclass

Each class offers two subclasses. At Level 1, you choose one and gain its Foundation Feature. As you advance, you will unlock its Specialization Feature (mid-levels) and Mastery Feature (high levels). See Book Three for the full subclass feature descriptions.

---

## Step 5: Assign Your Traits

Nexus 9 uses six Traits that define your character's physical and mental capabilities:

| Trait | Governs |
|:---|:---|
| **Agility** | Speed, reflexes, ranged accuracy, dodging |
| **Strength** | Raw physical power, melee force, endurance |
| **Finesse** | Precision, sleight of hand, stealth, lockpicking |
| **Instinct** | Awareness, gut feelings, survival, tracking |
| **Presence** | Charisma, intimidation, leadership, deception |
| **Knowledge** | Education, technology, medicine, history |

### Trait Generation: Standard Array

Assign the following values to your six Traits in any order:

**+2, +1, +1, 0, 0, -1**

This array ensures every character has clear strengths and a meaningful weakness. Your class description in Book Three recommends which Traits to prioritize, but you are free to assign them however you wish.

### Trait Generation: Point-Buy (Optional)

If your group prefers more customization, use the Point-Buy method:

1. All Traits start at **-1**.
2. You have **8 points** to spend.
3. Increasing a Trait by 1 costs 1 point (e.g., raising a Trait from -1 to 0 costs 1 point).
4. No Trait may exceed **+3** at character creation.
5. You must have at least one Trait at **-1 or lower** (representing a genuine weakness).

| Trait Value | Total Cost from -1 |
|:---:|:---:|
| -1 | 0 points |
| 0 | 1 point |
| +1 | 2 points |
| +2 | 3 points |
| +3 | 4 points |

---

## Step 6: Record Your Starting Statistics

Using your Class and Trait choices, record the following on your character sheet:

**Hit Points (HP):** Your starting HP is listed on your Class table (Step 3). This represents your physical resilience.

**Evasion:** Your starting Evasion is listed on your Class table. When an adversary attacks you, they must meet or exceed your Evasion on their attack roll. Add any bonuses from armor or features.

**Stress Slots:** All characters begin with **6 Stress Slots** (except the Mystic and Envoy, who begin with 7). Stress represents mental and emotional strain.

**Armor Slots:** Your starting Armor Slots are determined by your class (see the Class Damage Thresholds table in Book Four, Chapter 1).

**Damage Thresholds:** At Level 1, your unarmored damage thresholds are listed in the Class Damage Thresholds table (Book Four). If you are wearing armor, your armor's thresholds replace your base thresholds.

**Proficiency:** All characters begin at **Proficiency 1**. This determines how many damage dice you roll when you attack (1 die at Proficiency 1, 2 dice at Proficiency 2, etc.).

**Hope:** All characters begin each session with **2 Hope**. Hope is earned by rolling Hope on your action dice and is spent to activate class features and domain cards.

---

## Step 7: Choose Your Starting Experiences

Experiences are narrative skills that provide bonuses to specific types of action rolls. You begin with **three Experiences:**

1. **One Experience from your Community** (already assigned in Step 2, at +2).
2. **Two additional Experiences** of your choice, each at **+1**.

You may choose from the following list or create your own with GM approval:

| Experience | Example Uses |
|:---|:---|
| Bureaucracy | Navigating legal systems, filing permits, exploiting regulations |
| Deception | Lying convincingly, forging documents, maintaining a cover identity |
| Engineering | Repairing machines, understanding schematics, jury-rigging solutions |
| Intimidation | Threatening, interrogating, projecting authority |
| Investigation | Searching crime scenes, analyzing evidence, connecting clues |
| Medicine | Treating wounds, diagnosing illness, performing surgery |
| Negotiation | Brokering deals, mediating disputes, haggling prices |
| Persuasion | Convincing, charming, inspiring, rallying |
| Piloting | Flying ships, driving vehicles, navigating hazardous terrain |
| Street Smarts | Knowing the underworld, finding black markets, reading a crowd |
| Survival | Tracking, foraging, enduring harsh environments |
| Tactics | Reading a battlefield, planning ambushes, coordinating teams |
| Technology | Hacking terminals, operating sensors, understanding alien tech |
| Theology | Understanding religious customs, interpreting prophecy, recognizing rituals |

When you make an action roll that relates to one of your Experiences, add the Experience bonus to your roll.

---

## Step 8: Choose Your Starting Domain Cards

Domain cards are special abilities tied to your class. At Level 1, you choose **two Domain cards** — one from each of your class's two Domains. You may choose any Level 1 card from each Domain.

Your Domain cards form your **Loadout** — the abilities you have ready to use. At Level 1, your maximum Loadout is **5 cards**. Any additional cards you acquire through advancement are stored in your **Vault** and can be swapped into your Loadout during a Long Rest.

See Book Three for the complete Domain card lists for each class.

---

## Step 9: Choose Your Starting Equipment

All characters begin with the following standard-issue gear:

**Armor:** Choose one suit of Tier 1 armor from the Armor table in Book Four:
- Flight Suit (Light) — Minor 5 / Major 11 / Severe 20, Armor Score 3, +1 Evasion
- Light Combat Rig (Medium) — Minor 6 / Major 13 / Severe 23, Armor Score 3
- Marine Hardsuit (Heavy) — Minor 7 / Major 15 / Severe 26, Armor Score 4, -1 Evasion
- Assault Exoskeleton (Powered) — Minor 8 / Major 17 / Severe 29, Armor Score 4, -2 Evasion, -1 Agility

**Weapons:** Choose one Primary Weapon and one Secondary Weapon from the Tier 1 Weapon tables in Book Four. Your primary weapon should use a Trait you have invested in.

**Standard Kit:** Every character begins with the following items at no cost:
- Commlink (personal communicator, station-wide range)
- Datapad (portable computer, can access public networks)
- Emergency Med-Patch (x2) — Heal 1 HP as a Free Action; single use
- Ration Pack (x3) — One day of food and water
- Utility Knife (non-combat tool for cutting, prying, etc.)
- 200 Credits (the universal currency of the Known Galaxy)

**Class-Specific Starter Gear:**

| Class | Additional Starting Gear |
|:---|:---|
| **Envoy** | Diplomatic credentials (forged or real), formal attire, encrypted datapad |
| **Operative** | Lockpick set, holo-disguise emitter (1 use), smoke grenade (x2) |
| **Pilot** | Flight helmet with HUD, emergency beacon, star chart datapad |
| **Marine** | Extra ammo pack, combat stim (x1, clears 2 Stress), breaching charge |
| **Engineer** | Portable toolkit, welding torch, diagnostic scanner |
| **Mystic** | Meditation focus crystal, psi-dampener (blocks telepathic detection for 1 hour), journal |
| **Broker** | Concealed credit chip (500 Credits hidden), forged transit papers, encrypted commlink |
| **Medic** | Advanced med-kit (heals 1d6+2 HP, 3 uses), surgical tools, bioscanner |

---

## Step 10: Define Your Character

The final step is the most important: making your character a person, not just a collection of numbers.

**Name:** Choose a name that fits your Ancestry and background. See the Naming Guide in Book One for suggestions.

**Pronouns:** Declare your character's pronouns.

**Appearance:** Describe what your character looks like — their build, their clothing, their distinguishing features.

**Personality:** In one or two sentences, describe how your character acts. Are they cautious or reckless? Warm or cold? Idealistic or cynical?

**Motivation:** Why is your character on Nexus 9? What do they want more than anything? This should be a concrete, achievable goal — not "save the galaxy," but "find my missing sister" or "pay off my debt and buy a ship of my own."

**Fear:** What is your character most afraid of? This gives the GM a tool to create tension and gives you a source of dramatic moments.

---

## Character Creation Summary

For quick reference, here is the complete character creation process:

| Step | Action | Where to Find Details |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Choose Ancestry | This chapter |
| 2 | Choose Community | This chapter |
| 3 | Choose Class | Book Three |
| 4 | Choose Subclass | Book Three |
| 5 | Assign Traits (Standard Array or Point-Buy) | This chapter |
| 6 | Record Starting Statistics (HP, Evasion, Stress, Armor, Proficiency, Hope) | This chapter + Book Four |
| 7 | Choose 3 Starting Experiences | This chapter |
| 8 | Choose 2 Starting Domain Cards (1 per Domain) | Book Three |
| 9 | Choose Starting Equipment (Armor, Weapons, Standard Kit, Class Gear) | This chapter + Book Four |
| 10 | Define Name, Appearance, Personality, Motivation, Fear | This chapter |

sections.title: Building Your Agent
sections.content:
Creating a character in *Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* follows a structured, step-by-step process. Each step builds on the last, producing a fully realized operative ready to face the dangers of the Known Galaxy. This chapter walks you through every decision, from choosing your ancestry to recording your starting gear.

Before you begin, you will need a character sheet (see Appendix), two twelve-sided dice (2d12), and a set of standard polyhedral dice (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20).

---

sections.title: Step 1: Choose Your Ancestry
sections.content:
Your Ancestry represents the species or cultural lineage your character was born into. It determines your physical traits, your starting language, and one innate ability that reflects your heritage.

Nexus 9 features the following Ancestries:

| Ancestry | Description | Ancestry Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Terran** | Humans from the Coalition core worlds. Adaptable, ambitious, and widespread. | *Tenacity:* Once per Long Rest, when you fail an action roll, you may immediately reroll it and take the new result. |
| **Aurelian** | Tall, long-lived humanoids with gilded skin and a culture of imperial grandeur. | *Imperial Bearing:* You gain Advantage on Presence rolls when interacting with anyone who recognizes your noble heritage. |
| **Kaelen** | Stocky, resilient humanoids with mottled grey-green skin, adapted to harsh frontier worlds. | *Born Survivor:* You gain Advantage on Instinct rolls to resist environmental hazards (extreme cold, radiation, low oxygen). |
| **Valari** | Slender, crystalline-boned humanoids with luminous eyes and a deep spiritual tradition. | *Crystal Resonance:* You can sense the presence of Progenitor technology or Eclipse corruption within Close range without a roll. |
| **Mindclave-Born** | Terrans or Aurelians born with latent telepathic potential, identified and raised within the Mindclave. | *Psychic Sensitivity:* You can read the surface emotions (not thoughts) of any creature within Very Close range as a Free Action. |
| **Frontier-Born** | Mixed-heritage individuals from the unaligned border worlds — scrappy, resourceful, and self-reliant. | *Resourceful:* Once per Short Rest, you may produce a common, non-weapon item worth 50 Credits or less that you "happened to have." |
| **Synthetic** | Artificial beings — androids, uploaded consciousnesses, or bio-mechanical constructs. | *Machine Logic:* You are immune to telepathic intrusion and the Disoriented condition. You do not need to eat, drink, or breathe, but you require maintenance during Long Rests. |

---

sections.title: Step 2: Choose Your Community
sections.content:
Your Community represents where you grew up and the values that shaped you. It provides one Experience (a narrative skill bonus) and one Community Feature.

| Community | Description | Starting Experience | Community Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Core Worlder** | Raised on a wealthy, well-connected inner world. | Bureaucracy (+2) | *Connections:* You know the name and location of one mid-ranking official in any major faction. |
| **Station Rat** | Grew up on Nexus 9 or a similar orbital habitat. | Street Smarts (+2) | *Station Knowledge:* You always know the fastest route between any two points on a station, and you can identify safe houses and bolt-holes without a roll. |
| **Frontier Settler** | Raised on a remote colony world with little infrastructure. | Survival (+2) | *Self-Reliant:* During a Short Rest, you can repair one piece of damaged equipment without tools or an Engineer. |
| **Military Brat** | Grew up on Coalition military bases and warships. | Tactics (+2) | *Drilled:* In the first round of any combat, you act before any adversary, regardless of the spotlight order. |
| **Underbelly Dweller** | Raised in the criminal underworld of a major station. | Deception (+2) | *Underworld Contacts:* Once per session, you can locate a black-market dealer, fence, or informant in your current location without a roll. |
| **Temple Raised** | Raised in a Valari monastery or Aurelian religious order. | Theology (+2) | *Inner Peace:* Once per Long Rest, you may clear 2 Stress without spending Hope or taking a rest action. |
| **Exile** | You were cast out from your original community — a political dissident, a criminal, or a refugee. | Persuasion (+2) | *Nothing Left to Lose:* When you are at 3 or fewer HP, you gain +1 to all action rolls. |
| **Technoseer Apprentice** | Trained (briefly) by the Technoseers before leaving or being expelled. | Engineering (+2) | *Progenitor Intuition:* You gain Advantage on Knowledge rolls to identify, activate, or repair Progenitor technology. |

---

sections.title: Step 3: Choose Your Class
sections.content:
Your Class defines your core combat role, your primary abilities, and the Domain cards available to you. Nexus 9 features eight classes, each adapted from the Daggerheart core framework.

Choose one of the following classes. Full class descriptions, subclass features, and Domain card lists are found in Book Three.

| Class | Role | Domains | Starting HP | Starting Evasion |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Envoy** | Face / Social Specialist | Grace & Codex | 5 | 10 |
| **Operative** | Striker / Infiltrator | Midnight & Grace | 6 | 12 |
| **Pilot** | Skirmisher / Vehicle Specialist | Bone & Sage | 6 | 12 |
| **Marine** | Frontline Combatant | Blade & Bone | 6 | 11 |
| **Engineer** | Protector / Support Tank | Valor & Blade | 7 | 9 |
| **Mystic** | Ranged Damage / Telepathy | Arcana & Midnight | 6 | 10 |
| **Broker** | Utility / Controller | Sage & Arcana | 6 | 10 |
| **Medic** | Healer / Support | Splendor & Valor | 7 | 9 |

---

sections.title: Step 4: Choose Your Subclass
sections.content:
Each class offers two subclasses. At Level 1, you choose one and gain its Foundation Feature. As you advance, you will unlock its Specialization Feature (mid-levels) and Mastery Feature (high levels). See Book Three for the full subclass feature descriptions.

---

sections.title: Step 5: Assign Your Traits
sections.content:
Nexus 9 uses six Traits that define your character's physical and mental capabilities:

| Trait | Governs |
|:---|:---|
| **Agility** | Speed, reflexes, ranged accuracy, dodging |
| **Strength** | Raw physical power, melee force, endurance |
| **Finesse** | Precision, sleight of hand, stealth, lockpicking |
| **Instinct** | Awareness, gut feelings, survival, tracking |
| **Presence** | Charisma, intimidation, leadership, deception |
| **Knowledge** | Education, technology, medicine, history |

### Trait Generation: Standard Array

Assign the following values to your six Traits in any order:

**+2, +1, +1, 0, 0, -1**

This array ensures every character has clear strengths and a meaningful weakness. Your class description in Book Three recommends which Traits to prioritize, but you are free to assign them however you wish.

### Trait Generation: Point-Buy (Optional)

If your group prefers more customization, use the Point-Buy method:

1. All Traits start at **-1**.
2. You have **8 points** to spend.
3. Increasing a Trait by 1 costs 1 point (e.g., raising a Trait from -1 to 0 costs 1 point).
4. No Trait may exceed **+3** at character creation.
5. You must have at least one Trait at **-1 or lower** (representing a genuine weakness).

| Trait Value | Total Cost from -1 |
|:---:|:---:|
| -1 | 0 points |
| 0 | 1 point |
| +1 | 2 points |
| +2 | 3 points |
| +3 | 4 points |

---

sections.title: Step 6: Record Your Starting Statistics
sections.content:
Using your Class and Trait choices, record the following on your character sheet:

**Hit Points (HP):** Your starting HP is listed on your Class table (Step 3). This represents your physical resilience.

**Evasion:** Your starting Evasion is listed on your Class table. When an adversary attacks you, they must meet or exceed your Evasion on their attack roll. Add any bonuses from armor or features.

**Stress Slots:** All characters begin with **6 Stress Slots** (except the Mystic and Envoy, who begin with 7). Stress represents mental and emotional strain.

**Armor Slots:** Your starting Armor Slots are determined by your class (see the Class Damage Thresholds table in Book Four, Chapter 1).

**Damage Thresholds:** At Level 1, your unarmored damage thresholds are listed in the Class Damage Thresholds table (Book Four). If you are wearing armor, your armor's thresholds replace your base thresholds.

**Proficiency:** All characters begin at **Proficiency 1**. This determines how many damage dice you roll when you attack (1 die at Proficiency 1, 2 dice at Proficiency 2, etc.).

**Hope:** All characters begin each session with **2 Hope**. Hope is earned by rolling Hope on your action dice and is spent to activate class features and domain cards.

---

sections.title: Step 7: Choose Your Starting Experiences
sections.content:
Experiences are narrative skills that provide bonuses to specific types of action rolls. You begin with **three Experiences:**

1. **One Experience from your Community** (already assigned in Step 2, at +2).
2. **Two additional Experiences** of your choice, each at **+1**.

You may choose from the following list or create your own with GM approval:

| Experience | Example Uses |
|:---|:---|
| Bureaucracy | Navigating legal systems, filing permits, exploiting regulations |
| Deception | Lying convincingly, forging documents, maintaining a cover identity |
| Engineering | Repairing machines, understanding schematics, jury-rigging solutions |
| Intimidation | Threatening, interrogating, projecting authority |
| Investigation | Searching crime scenes, analyzing evidence, connecting clues |
| Medicine | Treating wounds, diagnosing illness, performing surgery |
| Negotiation | Brokering deals, mediating disputes, haggling prices |
| Persuasion | Convincing, charming, inspiring, rallying |
| Piloting | Flying ships, driving vehicles, navigating hazardous terrain |
| Street Smarts | Knowing the underworld, finding black markets, reading a crowd |
| Survival | Tracking, foraging, enduring harsh environments |
| Tactics | Reading a battlefield, planning ambushes, coordinating teams |
| Technology | Hacking terminals, operating sensors, understanding alien tech |
| Theology | Understanding religious customs, interpreting prophecy, recognizing rituals |

When you make an action roll that relates to one of your Experiences, add the Experience bonus to your roll.

---

sections.title: Step 8: Choose Your Starting Domain Cards
sections.content:
Domain cards are special abilities tied to your class. At Level 1, you choose **two Domain cards** — one from each of your class's two Domains. You may choose any Level 1 card from each Domain.

Your Domain cards form your **Loadout** — the abilities you have ready to use. At Level 1, your maximum Loadout is **5 cards**. Any additional cards you acquire through advancement are stored in your **Vault** and can be swapped into your Loadout during a Long Rest.

See Book Three for the complete Domain card lists for each class.

---

sections.title: Step 9: Choose Your Starting Equipment
sections.content:
All characters begin with the following standard-issue gear:

**Armor:** Choose one suit of Tier 1 armor from the Armor table in Book Four:
- Flight Suit (Light) — Minor 5 / Major 11 / Severe 20, Armor Score 3, +1 Evasion
- Light Combat Rig (Medium) — Minor 6 / Major 13 / Severe 23, Armor Score 3
- Marine Hardsuit (Heavy) — Minor 7 / Major 15 / Severe 26, Armor Score 4, -1 Evasion
- Assault Exoskeleton (Powered) — Minor 8 / Major 17 / Severe 29, Armor Score 4, -2 Evasion, -1 Agility

**Weapons:** Choose one Primary Weapon and one Secondary Weapon from the Tier 1 Weapon tables in Book Four. Your primary weapon should use a Trait you have invested in.

**Standard Kit:** Every character begins with the following items at no cost:
- Commlink (personal communicator, station-wide range)
- Datapad (portable computer, can access public networks)
- Emergency Med-Patch (x2) — Heal 1 HP as a Free Action; single use
- Ration Pack (x3) — One day of food and water
- Utility Knife (non-combat tool for cutting, prying, etc.)
- 200 Credits (the universal currency of the Known Galaxy)

**Class-Specific Starter Gear:**

| Class | Additional Starting Gear |
|:---|:---|
| **Envoy** | Diplomatic credentials (forged or real), formal attire, encrypted datapad |
| **Operative** | Lockpick set, holo-disguise emitter (1 use), smoke grenade (x2) |
| **Pilot** | Flight helmet with HUD, emergency beacon, star chart datapad |
| **Marine** | Extra ammo pack, combat stim (x1, clears 2 Stress), breaching charge |
| **Engineer** | Portable toolkit, welding torch, diagnostic scanner |
| **Mystic** | Meditation focus crystal, psi-dampener (blocks telepathic detection for 1 hour), journal |
| **Broker** | Concealed credit chip (500 Credits hidden), forged transit papers, encrypted commlink |
| **Medic** | Advanced med-kit (heals 1d6+2 HP, 3 uses), surgical tools, bioscanner |

---

sections.title: Step 10: Define Your Character
sections.content:
The final step is the most important: making your character a person, not just a collection of numbers.

**Name:** Choose a name that fits your Ancestry and background. See the Naming Guide in Book One for suggestions.

**Pronouns:** Declare your character's pronouns.

**Appearance:** Describe what your character looks like — their build, their clothing, their distinguishing features.

**Personality:** In one or two sentences, describe how your character acts. Are they cautious or reckless? Warm or cold? Idealistic or cynical?

**Motivation:** Why is your character on Nexus 9? What do they want more than anything? This should be a concrete, achievable goal — not "save the galaxy," but "find my missing sister" or "pay off my debt and buy a ship of my own."

**Fear:** What is your character most afraid of? This gives the GM a tool to create tension and gives you a source of dramatic moments.

---

sections.title: Character Creation Summary
sections.content:
For quick reference, here is the complete character creation process:

| Step | Action | Where to Find Details |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Choose Ancestry | This chapter |
| 2 | Choose Community | This chapter |
| 3 | Choose Class | Book Three |
| 4 | Choose Subclass | Book Three |
| 5 | Assign Traits (Standard Array or Point-Buy) | This chapter |
| 6 | Record Starting Statistics (HP, Evasion, Stress, Armor, Proficiency, Hope) | This chapter + Book Four |
| 7 | Choose 3 Starting Experiences | This chapter |
| 8 | Choose 2 Starting Domain Cards (1 per Domain) | Book Three |
| 9 | Choose Starting Equipment (Armor, Weapons, Standard Kit, Class Gear) | This chapter + Book Four |
| 10 | Define Name, Appearance, Personality, Motivation, Fear | This chapter |

chapters.title: Example of Play
chapters.content:
## A Scene Aboard Nexus 9

The following example demonstrates a complete scene of play in *Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark*, showing how the Hope and Fear economy, action rolls, damage thresholds, and the spotlight system work together at the table. This scene involves four players and a GM.

**The Crew:**
- **Kira** (Operative, played by Alex) — Agility +2, Finesse +1, Evasion 12, HP 6
- **Torren** (Marine, played by Sam) — Strength +2, Agility +1, Evasion 11, HP 6, wearing a Marine Hardsuit (Minor 7, Major 15, Severe 26, Armor Score 4)
- **Dr. Sable** (Medic, played by Jordan) — Knowledge +2, Presence +1, Evasion 9, HP 7
