| 5 | **Shape Material** | (Action) Spend 2 Hope. You use a fabricator to instantly create any non-weapon, non-armor item worth 1 Bag of Credits or less. |
| 5 | **Smite** | (Action) Mark 2 Stress. You overload a medical tool or weapon. Your next melee attack deals an additional 3d8 energy damage. |
| 6 | **Restoration** | (Action) Spend 3 Hope. You cure an ally of all diseases, toxins, and negative conditions. |
| 6 | **Zone of Protection** | (Action) Mark 3 Stress. You deploy a medical shield generator. Allies within Close range gain +2 to all defense rolls and Evasion. |
| 7 | **Splendor-Touched** | (Passive) Your healing abilities are miraculous. You gain a permanent +1 to your Presence trait, and your maximum Hope increases by 1. |
| 7 | **Healing Strike** | (Action) Mark 3 Stress. Make a melee attack. If it hits, you and all allies within Close range clear 2d6 HP. |
| 8 | **Shield Aura** | (Passive) You project a constant, low-level energy shield. You and all allies within Close range gain +1 Armor Score. |
| 8 | **Stunning Sunlight** | (Action) Spend 3 Hope. You unleash a blinding flash of radiation. All enemies within Far range are Blinded and take 2d8 energy damage. |
| 9 | **Overwhelming Aura** | (Action) Mark 4 Stress. You project an aura of absolute authority. Enemies cannot attack you or allies within Close range of you unless they pass a Difficulty 18 Presence roll. |
| 9 | **Salvation Beam** | (Action) Spend 4 Hope. You fire a concentrated beam of restorative nanites. One ally within Far range clears all marked HP and Stress. |
| 10 | **Invigoration** | (Passive) Allies within Far range of you cannot be reduced below 1 HP by any single attack. |
| 10 | **Resurrection** | (Action) Mark 5 Stress and spend 5 Hope. You use experimental, forbidden tech to bring a recently deceased ally back to life with 1 HP. |

*(The Medic also has access to the Valor Domain, detailed under the Engineer).*

---

chapters.content: *The Medic is a trauma surgeon and xenobiologist, essential for keeping the crew alive in a hostile galaxy.*

sections.title: The Vault and The Loadout
sections.content:
As characters level up in *Nexus 9*, they acquire new Domain Cards. However, a character cannot have every ability ready at all times.

*   **The Vault:** This is the collection of all Domain Cards your character has unlocked. Cards in your Vault cannot be used during a session.
*   **The Loadout:** This is your active hand of cards. You can only use the abilities printed on the cards currently in your Loadout.

sections.title: Managing Your Loadout
sections.content:
*   **Loadout Limit:** A character can have a maximum of **5 Domain Cards** in their active Loadout at any given time.
*   **Swapping Cards:** During a **Long Rest**, you can swap any number of cards between your Vault and your Loadout. During a **Short Rest**, you can swap exactly **one** card.
*   **Card Levels:** You can only place a card in your Loadout if your character's Level is equal to or higher than the card's Level.

sections.title: Card Types and Action Economy
sections.content:
Every Domain Card specifies when it can be used:
*   **(Action):** Using this card requires you to take the Spotlight and make an Action Roll. It consumes your turn in the action economy.
*   **(Reaction):** Using this card does not require the Spotlight. It is played in response to a specific trigger (e.g., "When you are attacked").
*   **(Passive):** This card provides a constant benefit as long as it is in your active Loadout. It does not require an action or a trigger to use.
*   **(Downtime):** This card can only be used during a Short or Long Rest, or during narrative downtime between missions.

sections.title: Costs
sections.content:
Many Domain Cards require a resource expenditure to activate:
*   **Spend Hope:** You must discard the specified number of Hope tokens from your character sheet. If you do not have enough Hope, you cannot use the card.
*   **Mark Stress:** You must mark the specified number of Stress boxes on your character sheet. If marking this Stress would cause you to exceed your maximum Stress, you take HP damage for the overflow as per the core rules.

---

chapters.title: 11. Cross-Class Domain Reference
chapters.content:
Because the 8 classes share the 9 Domains, players will often find themselves with access to the same pools of abilities. This encourages synergy and allows for diverse builds within the same class.

| Domain | Theme | Shared By |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Arcana** | Telepathy, Technomancy, Spacetime Manipulation | Mystic, Broker |
| **Blade** | Melee Superiority, Breaching, Physical Resilience | Marine, Engineer |
| **Bone** | Tactics, Evasion, Battlefield Positioning | Pilot, Marine |
| **Codex** | Law, Lore, Bureaucracy, System Manipulation | Envoy |
| **Grace** | Diplomacy, Deception, Social Engineering | Envoy, Operative |
| **Midnight** | Stealth, Sabotage, Assassination | Operative, Mystic |
| **Sage** | Survival, Logistics, Environmental Control | Pilot, Broker |
| **Splendor** | Medicine, Morale, Radiation/Light | Medic |
| **Valor** | Protection, Heavy Weapons, Holding the Line | Engineer, Medic |

*(Note: In the current 8-class structure, Codex and Splendor are exclusive to the Envoy and Medic, respectively. A future 9th class may share these domains).*

---

chapters.title: Domain Card Summary
chapters.content:
| Domain | Cards | Shared By |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Arcana** | 21 | Mystic, Broker |
| **Blade** | 21 | Marine, Engineer |
| **Bone** | 21 | Pilot, Marine |
| **Codex** | 21 | Envoy |
| **Grace** | 21 | Envoy, Operative |
| **Midnight** | 21 | Operative, Mystic |
| **Sage** | 21 | Pilot, Broker |
| **Splendor** | 21 | Medic |
| **Valor** | 21 | Engineer, Medic |
| **Total** | **189** | |

books.title: BOOK FOUR: THE ART OF WAR

chapters.title: How Combat Works
chapters.content:
## The Core Loop

Combat in *Nexus 9: The Fraying Dark* uses the Daggerheart action economy: there are no fixed initiative rounds. Instead, the spotlight passes between players and the GM based on Hope and Fear dice results. This chapter explains every step of resolving an attack, taking damage, and using armor — from the moment a trigger is pulled to the moment a character hits the deck.

---

## The Spotlight System

When combat begins, the GM describes the situation and asks, "Who acts first?" The table decides based on the fiction. If the crew was ambushed, the GM acts first (spending 1 Fear). If the crew initiated the fight, a player acts first.

After each action roll, the spotlight passes:

- **Hope result (Hope die higher or tied):** The acting player chooses who acts next — any player or the GM.
- **Fear result (Fear die higher):** The GM chooses who acts next — any adversary or any player.

This creates a dynamic, unpredictable flow where tactical coordination and narrative momentum matter more than a fixed turn order.

---

## Actions in Combat

On your turn in the spotlight, you may:

| Action Type | Description |
|:---|:---|
| **Action** | Attack, use a Domain Card, interact with the environment, attempt a skill roll, or use an item |
| **Movement** | Move one range band in any direction (free with your action) |
| **Free Action** | Speak a short sentence, draw or stow a weapon, press a button, drop an item |
| **Reaction** | Triggered by specific events (e.g., a defensive Domain Card). Costs 1 Hope unless the card says otherwise. |

You get **one Action and one Movement** per spotlight turn. Some Domain Cards or class features grant additional actions.

---

## Making an Attack

When you attack a target, follow these steps:

**Step 1: Declare your action.** State which weapon you are using, which target you are attacking, and whether you are using any Domain cards or features that modify the attack.

**Step 2: Roll 2d12.** This is your action roll. One die is your **Hope die** and the other is your **Fear die**. Before rolling, designate which is which (or use two different colors).

**Step 3: Add your Trait modifier.** Add the Trait listed on your weapon to the total of the two dice. For example, if you are firing a Mag-Pistol (Finesse) and your Finesse is +1, add +1 to the total.

**Step 4: Add any Experience bonus.** If you have a relevant Experience (e.g., Tactics for a called shot, Piloting for a vehicle-mounted weapon), add that bonus as well.

**Step 5: Compare to the target's Evasion.** If your total meets or exceeds the target's Evasion, the attack hits. If it is lower, the attack misses.

**Step 6: Determine Hope or Fear.** Regardless of whether the attack hits or misses, check which die rolled higher:
- If the **Hope die** is higher (or they are tied), the result is **Hope**. You gain 1 Hope token, and you choose who acts next.
- If the **Fear die** is higher, the result is **Fear**. The GM gains 1 Fear token and decides who acts next.

**Step 7: If the attack hits, roll damage.** Roll your weapon's damage dice. At Proficiency 1, you roll the weapon's listed dice once. At Proficiency 2, roll the dice twice and add both results. At Proficiency 3, roll three times, and so on. Add any flat damage bonuses listed on the weapon.

---

## The Damage Threshold System

Nexus 9 does **not** subtract damage from Hit Points directly. Instead, the total damage you roll is compared against the target's **Damage Thresholds** to determine the severity of the wound.

Every character and adversary has three thresholds: **Minor**, **Major**, and **Severe**.

### Damage Resolution Table

| Damage Total | Severity | HP Lost | Stress Marked |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Below the Minor threshold | **Glancing Blow** | 0 HP | 0 Stress |
| Meets or exceeds Minor | **Minor Hit** | 1 HP | 0 Stress |
| Meets or exceeds Major | **Major Hit** | 2 HP | 1 Stress |
| Meets or exceeds Severe | **Severe Hit** | 3 HP | 2 Stress |

> **Example:** Kira fires her Mag-Pistol (d6, Finesse) at a Syndicate Enforcer. She rolls a 5 on the d6 for a total of 5 damage. The Enforcer's thresholds are Minor 3 / Major 6 / Severe 12. Since 5 exceeds the Minor threshold but not the Major threshold, this is a Minor Hit — the Enforcer loses 1 HP and marks 0 Stress.

---

## Critical Hits and Critical Failures

When both dice show the same number (doubles), a critical event occurs. The outcome depends on whether the roll succeeds or fails:

**Critical Hit (doubles and the attack hits):** The severity of the damage increases by one step. A Glancing Blow becomes a Minor Hit. A Minor Hit becomes a Major Hit. A Major Hit becomes a Severe Hit. A Severe Hit that is critically enhanced deals 4 HP and 3 Stress.

**Critical Failure (doubles and the attack misses, or doubles on a failed non-combat roll):** The GM gains 2 Fear instead of 1, and the situation worsens dramatically — a weapon jams, an alarm triggers, cover is destroyed, or an ally is put in danger.

**Important:** When both dice show the same number, they are tied. Ties always go to Hope (the player generates 1 Hope and chooses who acts next). If the attack also hits, it is a Critical Hit. If the attack misses despite the tie, it is a Critical Failure — the player still generates 1 Hope, but the GM also gains 2 Fear and narrates a complication.

---

## Armor: How It Works

Armor in Nexus 9 provides two distinct benefits. Understanding the difference between them is essential.

### Damage Thresholds (from Armor)

When you wear armor, its **damage thresholds replace your base class thresholds**. Armor thresholds are always higher than unarmored thresholds, making you harder to wound. You use whichever set of thresholds is higher — your class base or your armor.

> **Example:** A Level 1 Operative has base thresholds of Minor 3 / Major 6 / Severe 12. If they wear a Flight Suit (Minor 5 / Major 11 / Severe 20), they use the armor's thresholds instead. A hit that deals 8 damage would be a Major Hit against the unarmored Operative but only a Minor Hit against the armored one.

### Armor Score and Armor Slots

**Armor Score** is a value printed on each suit of armor. It represents the number of **Armor Slots** available to you. Armor Slots are a consumable defensive resource — when you take a hit, you may **mark one Armor Slot** to reduce the severity of that hit by one step.

| Armor Type | Tier | Minor | Major | Severe | Armor Score (Slots) | Evasion Modifier |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Flight Suit (Light)** | 1 | 5 | 11 | 20 | 3 | +1 |
| **Tactical Vest (Medium)** | 1 | 6 | 13 | 23 | 3 | +0 |
| **Marine Hardsuit (Heavy)** | 1 | 7 | 15 | 26 | 4 | -1 |
| **Assault Exoskeleton (Powered)** | 2 | 8 | 17 | 29 | 4 | -1 |

> **Example:** Torren the Marine is wearing a Marine Hardsuit (Armor Score 4). He takes a Major Hit (2 HP, 1 Stress). He chooses to mark one of his 4 Armor Slots to reduce the severity by one step — the Major Hit becomes a Minor Hit (1 HP, 0 Stress). He now has 3 unmarked Armor Slots remaining.

**Clearing Armor Slots:** Armor Slots are restored during a Short Rest (restore half, rounded up) or a Long Rest (restore all). Some class features and Domain cards can also restore Armor Slots during combat.

**One Slot Per Hit:** You may only mark one Armor Slot per incoming attack unless a specific ability says otherwise.

### Strained and Broken Armor

When all Armor Slots are marked, your armor is **Strained**. You still benefit from the replacement thresholds, but you cannot mark any more slots until you rest. If you take a Severe Hit while Strained, the armor is **Broken** — it provides no benefits at all until repaired by an Engineer during downtime.

### Armor Summary

| Term | What It Does |
|:---|:---|
| **Damage Thresholds** (Minor/Major/Severe) | Determines how much HP and Stress you lose from a given damage total. Armor thresholds replace your base class thresholds. |
| **Armor Score** | The number of Armor Slots you have. Each slot can be marked once to reduce one hit's severity by one step. |
| **Armor Slots** | Consumable uses of your armor's protection. Cleared on rest. |

---

## Range Bands Defined

Nexus 9 uses abstract range bands rather than a measured grid. This keeps combat fast and cinematic while still providing tactical positioning.

### Range Band Definitions

| Range Band | Abstract Distance | Real-World Equivalent | Sci-Fi Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Melee** | Within arm's reach | 0–5 feet / 0–1.5 meters | Grappling in a corridor; fighting with a shock-pike |
| **Very Close** | A few steps away; the same room | 5–15 feet / 1.5–5 meters | Across a small cargo bay; opposite sides of an airlock |
| **Close** | Across a large room or down a corridor | 15–50 feet / 5–15 meters | Across the Promenade marketplace; down a station spoke |
| **Far** | Across a plaza or a large open area | 50–150 feet / 15–45 meters | Across a docking bay; from one end of a habitat ring to the other |
| **Very Far** | At the edge of visibility or sensor range | 150+ feet / 45+ meters | Across the exterior hull; sniper positions on a different ring |

### Movement

On your turn, you may move **one range band** as part of your action (e.g., from Far to Close, or from Close to Very Close). This movement is free and does not require a roll under normal circumstances.

**Difficult Terrain:** Moving through hazardous areas (debris fields, zero-gravity zones, active firefights) may require an Agility or Instinct roll. Failure means you do not move, or you move but suffer a consequence (taking damage, becoming Exposed, etc.).

**Dashing:** If you want to move **two range bands** in a single turn, you must spend your action to Dash. You cannot attack and Dash in the same turn unless a class feature or Domain card allows it.

**Disengaging:** If you are in Melee range of a hostile creature and want to move away, you must either:
- Spend 1 Hope to disengage safely, or
- Make an Agility roll against the enemy's Difficulty. On a failure, the enemy gets a free attack against you before you move.

### Weapon Range

Each weapon lists a maximum effective range. You can attack any target within that range or closer. Attacking at a range closer than the weapon's listed range has no penalty (a sniper rifle works fine at Close range — you just cannot use it in Melee).

You **cannot** attack a target beyond your weapon's listed range unless a specific ability allows it.

---

## Adversary Attacks

Adversaries do not roll to hit. Instead, the GM **spends Fear** to activate adversary actions:

| Action Cost | Examples |
|:---|:---|
| **1 Fear** | Standard attack, move, use a basic ability |
| **2 Fear** | Powerful ability, area attack, summon reinforcements |
| **3 Fear** | Ultimate ability, environmental catastrophe, boss phase transition |

When an adversary attacks, the **target player rolls to defend** (2d12 + Agility vs. the adversary's Attack Modifier as the Difficulty). If the player fails, the adversary's listed damage is applied to the player's thresholds. If the player succeeds, the attack misses.

This keeps all dice rolling in the players' hands and maintains the Hope/Fear economy on every roll.

---

## Being Downed and Death Moves

When your HP reaches 0, you are **Downed**. You collapse, drop what you are holding, and cannot take actions. You are not dead — yet.

**Stabilization:** An ally can stabilize you by spending an action and succeeding on a Knowledge roll (Difficulty 12). A stabilized character regains 1 HP and is conscious but Weakened (Disadvantage on all rolls until a Short Rest).

**Death Move:** If you are Downed and no ally stabilizes you before the GM's next action, you must make your class's **Death Move** — a dramatic, final choice unique to each class. Death Moves are not saving throws. They are narrative moments where you choose how your character faces the end.

Each class's Death Move is detailed in Book Three. Death Moves always offer a choice between two options — one that saves the character at a terrible cost, and one that sacrifices the character for a powerful final effect.

---

## Rest and Recovery

| Rest Type | Duration | HP Restored | Stress Cleared | Armor Slots Restored | Other |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Short Rest** | ~1 hour, safe location | Half max HP (rounded up) | 1 Stress | Half Armor Slots (rounded up) | Clear most conditions |
| **Long Rest** | ~8 hours, secure location | All HP | All Stress | All Armor Slots | Clear all conditions, reset daily abilities |

**Interrupted Rest:** If a rest is interrupted by combat or a crisis, the characters gain no benefit. The GM may allow a partial benefit (restore 1 HP) if the interruption came near the end of the rest period.

---

## Putting It All Together: Attack Resolution Example

> **Situation:** Voss the Marine (Strength +2, Proficiency 1) attacks a Kaelen Enforcer with his Vibro-Sword (Strength, Melee, d10+3 kinetic, Two-Handed). The Enforcer has Evasion 10, and thresholds of Minor 3 / Major 6 / Severe 12.
>
> **Step 1:** Voss declares he is attacking the Enforcer in Melee with his Vibro-Sword.
>
> **Step 2:** Voss rolls 2d12. He gets a 7 (Hope die) and a 4 (Fear die). Total: 11.
>
> **Step 3:** Add Strength (+2). Total: 13.
>
> **Step 4:** 13 exceeds the Enforcer's Evasion of 10. The attack hits.
>
> **Step 5:** Hope die (7) is higher than Fear die (4). This is a Hope result. Voss gains 1 Hope and chooses who acts next.
>
> **Step 6:** Voss rolls damage. Vibro-Sword deals d10+3. He rolls a 7 on the d10, plus 3 = 10 total damage.
>
> **Step 7:** Compare 10 to the Enforcer's thresholds. 10 exceeds Major (6) but not Severe (12). This is a Major Hit. The Enforcer loses 2 HP and marks 1 Stress.
>
> **Step 8:** The Enforcer has no Armor Slots to mark, so the damage stands.

---

> *"Combat is not a math problem. It is a story about people under pressure, making impossible choices, and living with the consequences."*

| Rest Type | Duration | HP Restored | Stress Cleared | Armor Slots Restored | Other |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Short Rest** | ~1 hour, safe location | Half max HP (rounded up) | 1 Stress | Half Armor Slots (rounded up) | Clear most conditions |
| **Long Rest** | ~8 hours, secure location | All HP | All Stress | All Armor Slots | Clear all conditions, reset daily abilities |

**Interrupted Rest:** If a rest is interrupted by combat or a crisis, the characters gain no benefit. The GM may allow a partial benefit (restore 1 HP) if the interruption came near the end of the rest period.

---

sections.title: Putting It All Together: Attack Resolution Example
sections.content:
> **Situation:** Voss the Marine (Strength +2, Proficiency 1) attacks a Kaelen Enforcer with his Vibro-Sword (Strength, Melee, d10+3 kinetic, Two-Handed). The Enforcer has Evasion 10, and thresholds of Minor 3 / Major 6 / Severe 12.
>
> **Step 1:** Voss declares he is attacking the Enforcer in Melee with his Vibro-Sword.
>
> **Step 2:** Voss rolls 2d12. He gets a 7 (Hope die) and a 4 (Fear die). Total: 11.
>
> **Step 3:** Add Strength (+2). Total: 13.
>
> **Step 4:** 13 exceeds the Enforcer's Evasion of 10. The attack hits.
>
> **Step 5:** Hope die (7) is higher than Fear die (4). This is a Hope result. Voss gains 1 Hope and chooses who acts next.
>
> **Step 6:** Voss rolls damage. Vibro-Sword deals d10+3. He rolls a 7 on the d10, plus 3 = 10 total damage.
>
> **Step 7:** Compare 10 to the Enforcer's thresholds. 10 exceeds Major (6) but not Severe (12). This is a Major Hit. The Enforcer loses 2 HP and marks 1 Stress.
>
> **Step 8:** The Enforcer has no Armor Slots to mark, so the damage stands.

---

> *"Combat is not a math problem. It is a story about people under pressure, making impossible choices, and living with the consequences."*

chapters.title: Design Philosophy: Damage and Armor
chapters.content:
> *A note from the designers on how and why Nexus 9's damage system differs from the standard Daggerheart model.*

---

## Why Fixed HP Loss?

The Daggerheart SRD uses a proportional damage threshold system where Major hits cost half your maximum HP and Severe hits cost all remaining HP. This creates an extremely lethal combat environment where a single unlucky roll can take a character from full health to unconscious.

Nexus 9 deliberately uses a **fixed HP loss model** (0/1/2/3) for the following reasons:

**1. Campaign Longevity.** A 12-chapter campaign with escalating stakes requires characters who can survive multiple combats per session. The proportional model produces a "rocket tag" dynamic where whoever hits first wins — exciting for one-shots, but exhausting over a 40-session campaign.

**2. Meaningful Armor.** In the proportional model, armor is binary: either you negate the hit entirely (by spending an Armor Slot) or you take devastating damage. In the fixed model, armor provides a gradient of protection — higher thresholds mean fewer hits register at all, and Armor Slots let you downgrade hits that do land. This makes armor selection a genuine tactical choice rather than a simple "how many free passes do I get?"

**3. Stress as a Secondary Resource.** By tying Stress to Major and Severe hits (rather than to HP loss), we create a dual-track attrition system. A character can be physically healthy but psychologically shattered, or vice versa. This produces the kind of dramatic tension — "I am fine, I am FINE" — that drives character-focused play.

**4. Predictable Math for GMs.** When the GM knows that a Tier 2 adversary dealing 12 damage will always cost a lightly-armored character exactly 2 HP and 1 Stress, encounter design becomes intuitive. The GM can gauge threat levels at a glance without running probability calculations.

---

## How Armor Works (And Why It Is Not Subtraction)

Many sci-fi RPGs use a "subtract armor from damage" model. Nexus 9 does not, for two reasons:

**1. Subtraction creates a "damage floor" problem.** If your armor is 5 and the enemy deals 6 damage, you take 1 HP — but if the enemy deals 5, you take 0. This creates a binary cliff where armor either negates damage entirely or barely matters. The threshold model avoids this by making armor raise the bar for what counts as a meaningful hit.

**2. Armor Slots create dramatic choices.** When you take a Major Hit, you can spend an Armor Slot to reduce it to a Minor Hit. But you only have 3-4 slots per rest. Do you spend one now, or save it for the ambush you suspect is coming? This is the kind of resource management that produces memorable table moments.

---

## Compatibility Note

These modifications are designed to be compatible with the broader Daggerheart ecosystem. If your table prefers the standard proportional damage model, you can use it with Nexus 9 by:

1. Replacing the fixed HP loss table with the standard SRD thresholds (Below Minor = 1 HP; Minor = 1 HP + 1 Stress; Major = half max HP rounded up; Severe = all remaining HP)
2. Treating Armor Score as a damage ceiling for slot-based negation rather than a slot count
3. Adjusting adversary damage values downward by approximately 30% to compensate for the increased lethality

We recommend playtesting both models with your group and choosing the one that best fits your preferred level of danger.

---

> *"The system serves the story. If the math does not produce drama, change the math."*
> — Nexus 9 Design Principle

> *"The system serves the story. If the math does not produce drama, change the math."*
> — Nexus 9 Design Principle

chapters.title: 1. How Damage Works in Nexus 9
chapters.content:
When a character is hit by an attack, the total damage rolled is compared against that character's **Damage Thresholds** to determine how many Hit Points (HP) they actually lose. Damage is not subtracted directly from HP. Instead, the severity of the hit determines the cost.

> **Below Minor Threshold:** The hit stings but causes no lasting harm. The character loses **0 HP**. The GM may describe a glancing blow, a near-miss that rattles the character, or armor absorbing the impact.
>
> **Meets or Exceeds Minor Threshold:** The hit draws blood or causes real pain. The character loses **1 HP**.
>
> **Meets or Exceeds Major Threshold:** A serious wound. The character loses **2 HP** and must **mark 1 Stress**.
>
> **Meets or Exceeds Severe Threshold:** A devastating blow that could be fatal. The character loses **3 HP** and must **mark 2 Stress**.

**Armor Slots** provide additional protection. When a character takes damage that would cost them HP, they may choose to **mark an Armor Slot** to reduce the severity by one threshold (e.g., a Major hit becomes a Minor hit, costing 1 HP instead of 2). Characters can only mark one Armor Slot per incoming attack unless a specific ability says otherwise.

---

chapters.title: 2. Class Damage Thresholds

sections.title: Tier 1 (Levels 1-2)
sections.content:
|Class|HP|Evasion|Minor|Major|Severe|Stress Slots|Armor Slots|Role|
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Marine** | 6 | 11 | 4 | 8 | 15 | 6 | 3 | Frontline combatant |
| **Engineer** | 7 | 9 | 5 | 9 | 16 | 5 | 4 | Protector / support tank |
| **Medic** | 7 | 9 | 4 | 8 | 14 | 5 | 4 | Healer / support |
| **Operative** | 6 | 12 | 3 | 6 | 12 | 6 | 2 | Striker / infiltrator |
| **Pilot** | 6 | 12 | 3 | 7 | 13 | 6 | 2 | Skirmisher / vehicle specialist |
| **Broker** | 6 | 10 | 3 | 7 | 13 | 6 | 3 | Utility / controller |
| **Mystic** | 6 | 10 | 2 | 5 | 11 | 7 | 2 | Ranged damage / telepathy |
| **Envoy** | 5 | 10 | 2 | 6 | 11 | 7 | 2 | Face / social specialist |

sections.title: Design Notes
sections.content:
The Marine and Engineer are the toughest characters, with the highest thresholds and armor slots. They are designed to stand in the line of fire. The Operative and Pilot survive through high Evasion — they are hard to hit but fragile when struck. The Mystic and Envoy have the lowest thresholds but the most Stress slots, reflecting their reliance on abilities rather than physical endurance. The Medic is a hybrid — moderately tough with strong support capabilities.

---

chapters.content: The following tables define the base Damage Thresholds for all 8 classes across the 4 Tiers of play. These thresholds assume the character is **unarmored**. Armor provides its own thresholds that replace these base values when worn.

chapters.title: 3. Armor System

sections.title: Tier 1 Armor (Starting Equipment)
sections.content:
| Armor | Nexus 9 Name | Minor | Major | Severe | Armor Score | Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Light | Flight Suit | 5 | 11 | 20 | 3 | *Flexible:* +1 to Evasion |
| Medium | Light Combat Rig | 6 | 13 | 23 | 3 | -- |
| Heavy | Marine Hardsuit | 7 | 15 | 26 | 4 | *Heavy:* -1 to Evasion |
| Powered | Assault Exoskeleton | 8 | 17 | 29 | 4 | *Very Heavy:* -2 to Evasion; -1 to Agility |

sections.title: Tier 2 Armor
sections.content:
| Armor | Nexus 9 Name | Minor | Major | Severe | Armor Score | Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Light | Reinforced Flight Suit | 7 | 16 | 28 | 4 | *Flexible:* +1 to Evasion |
| Medium | Tactical Combat Rig | 9 | 20 | 35 | 4 | -- |
| Heavy | Mk.II Marine Hardsuit | 11 | 24 | 42 | 5 | *Heavy:* -1 to Evasion |
| Powered | Heavy Assault Exo | 13 | 28 | 49 | 5 | *Very Heavy:* -2 to Evasion; -1 to Agility |
| Special | Valari Energy Mesh | 9 | 21 | 37 | 4 | *Warded:* Reduce incoming energy damage by your Armor Score before applying it to your damage thresholds. |
| Special | Kaelen Scrapplate | 9 | 21 | 37 | 4 | *Resilient:* Before you mark your last Armor Slot, roll a d6. On a result of 6, reduce the severity by one threshold without marking an Armor Slot. |
| Special | Coalition Ballistic Vest | 9 | 20 | 35 | 4 | *Reinforced:* When you mark your last Armor Slot, increase your damage thresholds by +2 until you clear at least 1 Armor Slot. |
| Special | Aurelian Ceremonial Guard | 8 | 18 | 32 | 5 | *Quiet:* You gain a +2 bonus to rolls you make to move silently. |

sections.content:

| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |

| Powered | Siege Exoskeleton | 15 | 35 | 61 | 6 | *Very Heavy:* -2 to Evasion; -1 to Agility |
| Special | Aurelian Gilded Plate | 11 | 27 | 47 | 5 | *Gilded:* +1 to Presence |
| Special | Eclipse-Warded Suit | 11 | 27 | 47 | 5 | *Impenetrable:* Once per short rest, when you would mark your last Hit Point, you can instead mark a Stress. |
| Special | Spiked Boarding Armor | 10 | 25 | 44 | 5 | *Sharp:* On a successful attack against a target within Melee range, add a d4 to the damage roll. |

sections.title: Tier 4 Armor
sections.content:
| Armor | Nexus 9 Name | Minor | Major | Severe | Armor Score | Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Light | Legendary Flight Suit | 11 | 32 | 56 | 6 | *Flexible:* +1 to Evasion |
| Medium | Legendary Combat Rig | 13 | 36 | 63 | 6 | -- |

---

chapters.content: Armor in *Nexus 9* provides two benefits: it raises the character's Damage Thresholds (making them harder to wound) and provides Armor Slots (which can be marked to reduce the severity of a hit). Armor thresholds **replace** the character's base thresholds when worn.

chapters.title: 4. The Action Economy — How Combat Flows

sections.title: The GM Action Tracker
sections.content:
At the start of a scene, the GM places a number of **Fear tokens** on the Action Tracker based on the scene's complexity:

| Scene Type | Starting Fear | Examples |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Incidental | 0-1 | A tense conversation that might turn violent; a minor customs inspection. |
| Minor | 1-3 | A bar fight in the Underbelly; a brief skirmish with pirates during docking. |
| Standard | 2-4 | A firefight in a station corridor; a tense standoff with Mindclave agents. |
| Major | 4-8 | A full boarding action; a battle with an Eclipse Void-Shape; a political assassination attempt. |
| Climactic | 6-12 | The final assault on the Eclipse Nexus; the defense of the Grand Assembly during a coup. |

The GM gains additional Fear tokens whenever a player rolls **Fear** on their action dice (the lower of the 2d12 is higher). The GM **spends** Fear tokens to activate adversary actions, trigger environmental hazards, or introduce complications.

sections.title: What a Player Can Do
sections.content:
When a player has the **spotlight**, they may do the following:

**One Action.** The player's primary contribution to the scene. This includes attacking, using a Domain card, attempting a skill check, providing aid to an ally, or interacting with the environment. The player describes what they want to do and rolls 2d12 + the relevant Trait.

**Movement.** The player may move one range band (e.g., from Far to Close, or from Close to Very Close) as part of their action. Moving through dangerous terrain or past enemies may require an additional roll.

**A Free Action.** Brief actions that do not require a roll, such as speaking a short sentence, drawing a weapon, or pressing a button. The GM determines what qualifies as a free action.

sections.title: Passing the Spotlight
sections.content: After a player resolves their action, the spotlight passes. On a **Hope result** (the higher d12 is the Hope die), the player chooses which ally receives the spotlight next. On a **Fear result** (the higher d12 is the Fear die), the GM gains a Fear token and decides who acts next — this could be another player or an adversary.

sections.title: GM Turns
sections.content:
The GM does not have a fixed turn. Instead, the GM **spends Fear tokens** to activate adversary actions at any point during the scene. Each adversary action costs Fear:

| Adversary Action | Fear Cost |
| :--- | :--- |
| Standard Attack (one adversary attacks one target) | 1 Fear |
| Move (one adversary moves one range band) | 0 Fear (free with an attack) |
| Special Ability (use a named ability from the adversary's stat block) | 1-3 Fear (listed on the stat block) |
| Environmental Hazard (trigger a trap, explosion, or structural collapse) | 1-2 Fear |
| Reinforcements (a new group of minions arrives) | 2-4 Fear |
| Escalation (the situation gets dramatically worse — alarms sound, a hull breach opens, a hostage is taken) | 3-5 Fear |

sections.title: Range Bands
sections.content:
*Nexus 9* uses abstract range bands rather than a grid. This keeps combat fast and cinematic.

| Range Band | Description | Sci-Fi Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Melee** | Within arm's reach. | Grappling in a corridor; fighting with a shock-pike. |
| **Very Close** | A few steps away; the same room. | Across a small cargo bay; opposite sides of an airlock. |
| **Close** | Across a large room or down a corridor. | Across the Promenade marketplace; down a station spoke. |
| **Far** | Across a plaza or a large open area. | Across a docking bay; from one end of a habitat ring to the other. |
| **Very Far** | At the edge of visibility or sensor range. | Across the exterior hull; sniper positions on a different ring. |

---

chapters.content: *Nexus 9* does not use traditional initiative. Instead, combat uses a **fluid spotlight system** driven by the **Hope and Fear economy**.

sections.title: Stress
sections.content:
**Stress** represents mental and emotional strain — fear, exhaustion, psychic pressure, and moral compromise. Characters mark Stress when they:

- Take a Major or Severe hit (as described in the Damage Threshold rules above)
- Use certain powerful Domain cards or telepathic abilities
- Witness something horrifying or morally devastating
- Push themselves beyond their limits (e.g., sprinting through a firefight, staying awake for days)

When a character **marks their last Stress slot**, they reach their **Breaking Point**. They must immediately choose one of the following:

| Breaking Point Option | Effect |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Lash Out** | The character acts recklessly. They gain Advantage on their next attack but all enemies gain Advantage on attacks against them until the end of the scene. |
| **Shut Down** | The character freezes or withdraws. They cannot take actions until an ally spends 1 Hope to snap them out of it, or until the scene ends. |
| **Compromise** | The character makes a desperate bargain — they agree to something they would normally refuse, reveal a secret, or abandon an objective. The GM and player negotiate the terms. |

After reaching a Breaking Point, the character **clears half their Stress slots** (rounded up) and continues.

sections.title: Conditions
sections.content:
Conditions are specific debuffs applied by adversary abilities, environmental hazards, or failed rolls.

| Condition | Effect | Clearing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pinned Down** | You cannot move from your current position. You have Disadvantage on attack rolls. | Spend 1 Hope, or an ally provides covering fire (uses their action). |
| **Blinded** | You have Disadvantage on all rolls that require sight. You cannot make attacks beyond Very Close range. | Clears at the end of the scene, or when the source is removed. |
| **Exposed** | Your Evasion is reduced by 2. Enemies have Advantage on attacks against you. | Move to cover (requires movement), or an ally uses Shield-Bearer or equivalent. |
| **Restrained** | You cannot move. You have Disadvantage on Agility and Finesse rolls. | Succeed on a Strength roll (Difficulty 13), or an ally frees you. |
| **Disoriented** | You have Disadvantage on Knowledge and Instinct rolls. You cannot use telepathic abilities. | Clears at the end of your next action, or when an ally spends 1 Hope. |
| **Wounded** | Your damage thresholds are reduced by 2 at all levels. This condition persists until you take a long rest or receive medical treatment. | Long rest, or a Medic uses Combat Triage or equivalent. |
| **Compromised** | Your communications are being monitored. Any plans you discuss openly are known to the enemy. | Succeed on a Knowledge roll (Difficulty 15) to sweep for bugs, or change your comm frequency (requires an Engineer). |

---

sections.title: Short Rest
sections.content:
A **Short Rest** represents a brief pause — catching your breath behind cover, patching a wound in a maintenance corridor, or regrouping in a safe room. A Short Rest takes approximately **30 minutes to 1 hour** of in-game time.

During a Short Rest, each character may:

- **Clear up to half their marked Stress slots** (rounded up).
- **Clear 1 HP** (representing basic first aid and adrenaline recovery).
- **Clear all Armor Slots** (representing field repairs to armor and equipment).
- **Perform one Short Rest action** from the following list:

| Short Rest Action | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Field Repair** | Repair a damaged piece of equipment or weapon. Requires an Engineer or appropriate tools. |
| **Patch Up** | A Medic can heal an ally for an additional 1d6 HP. |
| **Gather Intel** | Make a Knowledge or Instinct roll to analyze the situation and gain information about the next encounter. |
| **Secure the Area** | Make an Instinct roll to set up a defensive position, granting +1 Evasion to all allies in the area for the next encounter. |
| **Recall a Domain Card** | Pay the Recall Cost (in Hope) to retrieve a Domain card from your Vault and add it to your active Loadout. |

sections.title: Long Rest
sections.content:
A **Long Rest** represents a full recovery period — sleeping in quarters, receiving proper medical treatment in a med-bay, or spending a full day in port. A Long Rest takes approximately **8 hours** of in-game time and requires a safe location.

During a Long Rest, each character:

- **Clears all HP.**
- **Clears all Stress.**
- **Clears all Armor Slots.**
- **Clears all Conditions** (except permanent injuries or scars).
- **May perform two Downtime Moves** from the following list:

| Downtime Move | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Work a Contact** | Reach out to an NPC in your network. Make a Presence roll to gain a favor, a piece of information, or a lead. |
| **Forge Documents** | Create false identification, transit papers, or diplomatic credentials. Requires a Finesse roll. |
| **Decrypt Data** | Analyze a data chip, intercepted transmission, or encrypted file. Requires a Knowledge roll. |
| **Upgrade Equipment** | Spend Credits to improve a weapon or piece of armor to the next tier (requires appropriate facilities and an Engineer). |
| **Synthesize Compounds** | A Medic can craft medical supplies (Combat Stims, Antidotes, Neural Stabilizers) using available materials. |
| **Train** | Practice a specific skill. Gain a +1 bonus to your next roll using a specific Trait (this bonus is consumed after one use). |
| **Repair the Ship** | Clear System Stress from the crew's ship. Requires an Engineer and appropriate facilities. |
| **Investigate the Station** | Explore a specific area of Nexus 9. The GM provides a discovery, a rumor, or a complication based on the area explored. |
| **Lay Low** | Reduce your visibility. If any faction is actively searching for you, their progress is delayed by one step on their Threat Clock. |
| **Carouse** | Spend time in the Promenade or Underbelly. Make a Presence roll — on a success, you hear a useful rumor or make a new contact. On a failure, you attract unwanted attention. |

---

chapters.title: 7. Death and Dying

sections.title: Scars
sections.content:
Scars are permanent consequences of near-death experiences. They are primarily narrative, but the GM and player should agree on a minor mechanical effect that reflects the scar. Examples:

| Scar | Narrative Effect | Mechanical Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lost Eye | Depth perception is compromised. | -1 to ranged attack rolls beyond Far range. |
| Cybernetic Arm | A mechanical replacement for a lost limb. | +1 to Strength rolls involving that arm; -1 to Finesse rolls requiring fine motor control. |
| Psychic Scarring | A telepathic attack left lasting damage. | -1 to resist telepathic intrusion; +1 to detect telepathic activity (you recognize the sensation). |
| Survivor's Guilt | You lost someone you could not save. | -1 to rolls when an ally is dying; +1 to rolls to protect allies from harm. |
| Respiratory Damage | Exposure to vacuum or toxic atmosphere. | Disadvantage on rolls requiring sustained physical exertion in low-oxygen environments. |

---

chapters.content:
When a character **marks their last HP**, they fall **unconscious** and begin dying. On each subsequent GM turn (or when the GM spends 1 Fear), the dying character must roll a **Death Move**:

**Roll 2d12.**

| Result | Outcome |
| :--- | :--- |
| **19+** | You stabilize. You are unconscious but no longer dying. You regain 1 HP when the scene ends. |
| **12-18** | You cling to life. Nothing changes. You must roll again on the next GM turn. |
| **6-11** | You slip closer to death. Mark 1 Stress (even though you are unconscious). If you have no Stress slots remaining, you die. |
| **2-5** | You are on the edge. You gain a **Scar** (a permanent narrative mark — a missing eye, a cybernetic replacement, a recurring nightmare, a phobia). You stabilize but are permanently changed. |

An ally can attempt to **stabilize** a dying character by spending their action and succeeding on a Knowledge roll (Difficulty 13). A Medic gains Advantage on this roll. Success stabilizes the character at 1 HP.

chapters.title: 8. Ship-Scale Damage Thresholds
chapters.content:
Starship combat uses the same threshold system as personal combat, but at a larger scale. Ship damage thresholds are determined by the ship's **Frame Class**, not by the crew.

| Frame Class | Hull Points | Minor | Major | Severe | System Stress Slots |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Fighter** | 8-10 | 3 | 6 | 12 | 4 |
| **Courier** | 15-20 | 5 | 10 | 18 | 6 |
| **Freighter** | 25-30 | 7 | 14 | 25 | 8 |
| **Gunship** | 35-40 | 9 | 18 | 32 | 8 |
| **Cruiser** | 60-80 | 12 | 25 | 44 | 10 |
| **Capital** | 100-120 | 18 | 36 | 63 | 12 |

When a ship's **System Stress** is maxed out, the GM rolls on the **Critical System Failure Table**:

| Roll (1d12) | Critical System Failure |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1-2 | **Weapons Offline.** All weapon systems are disabled until an Engineer succeeds on a Knowledge roll (Difficulty 15) to restore them. |
| 3-4 | **Engines Stall.** The ship cannot move until an Engineer succeeds on a Knowledge roll (Difficulty 13) to restart the engines. The ship is a sitting target. |
| 5-6 | **Shields Collapse.** The ship's damage thresholds are halved (round down) until an Engineer reroutes power (requires 2 actions). |
| 7-8 | **Hull Breach.** All characters in the affected section must succeed on an Agility roll (Difficulty 13) or be Exposed. The breach must be sealed (Engineer action, Difficulty 15) or the section is vented to space in 3 rounds. |
| 9-10 | **Life Support Failing.** All characters on the ship begin marking 1 Stress per round until an Engineer restores life support (Difficulty 17). |
| 11 | **Navigation Offline.** The ship cannot make hyperspace jumps or use advanced sensors until repaired (Engineer, Difficulty 15). If in hyperspace, the ship drops out at a random location. |
| 12 | **Catastrophic Failure.** The ship is crippled. All systems go offline simultaneously. The crew has 1d4 rounds to abandon ship or attempt an emergency repair (Engineer, Difficulty 20). Failure means the ship is destroyed. |

---

chapters.title: Quick Reference: Damage Resolution
chapters.content:
**Step 1:** The attacker rolls their weapon's damage dice (e.g., 1d8+3 for a Plasma Carbine).

**Step 2:** Compare the total damage to the target's Damage Thresholds (from their armor, or their base class thresholds if unarmored).

**Step 3:** Determine the severity:

| Damage Total vs. Thresholds | HP Lost | Stress Marked |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Below Minor | 0 HP | 0 Stress |
| Meets/Exceeds Minor | 1 HP | 0 Stress |
| Meets/Exceeds Major | 2 HP | 1 Stress |
| Meets/Exceeds Severe | 3 HP | 2 Stress |

**Step 4:** The target may **mark an Armor Slot** to reduce the severity by one step (e.g., Major becomes Minor: 1 HP instead of 2, no Stress).

**Step 5:** Apply the damage. If the target's last HP is marked, they fall unconscious and begin dying.

chapters.title: 1. Weapon Systems
chapters.content:
Weapons in *Nexus 9* are categorized by the primary **Trait** used to wield them effectively. Every weapon also specifies a **Range**, a **Damage** value (rolled on a successful attack), a **Burden** (One-Handed or Two-Handed), and an optional **Feature** that modifies how the weapon behaves in combat.

The six Traits govern which weapons a character naturally favors:

| Trait | Weapon Archetype | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Agility** | Precision firearms, balanced melee blades | Mag-Pistol, Vibro-Sword, Sniper Rifle |
| **Strength** | Heavy ordnance, breaching tools, massive melee weapons | Gravity Hammer, Breaching Axe, Boarding Halberd |
| **Finesse** | Concealable sidearms, sniper systems, tactical blades | Holdout Blaster, Concealed Shiv, Hand Cannon |
| **Instinct** | Scatterguns, survival tools, intuitive tech | Scattergun, Survival Staff, Twin Blasters |
| **Presence** | Officer sidearms, dueling blades, intimidation weapons | Ceremonial Rapier, Officer's Cutlass, Diplomat's Scepter |
| **Knowledge** | Experimental tech, directed energy weapons, hacking tools | Hacking Wand, Heavy Plasma Caster, Fusion Gloves |

Damage types are broadly categorized as **Kinetic** (slugs, blades, explosives) or **Energy** (plasma, laser, shock, sonic). Some armors or adversaries may have specific resistances or vulnerabilities to one type. When a weapon deals "kinetic or energy" damage, the attacker chooses which type to apply on each successful attack.

---

chapters.content: These are standard-issue weapons available to starting characters, security forces, and mercenaries across Nexus 9. They represent the baseline of modern galactic armament — reliable, mass-produced, and widely available.

chapters.title: 6. Weapon Features Glossary
chapters.content:
The following is a complete alphabetical reference of all weapon features used in *Nexus 9*.

| Feature | Effect |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Barrier** | Bonus to Armor Score; -1 to Evasion. |
| **Bonded** | Gain a bonus to your damage rolls equal to your level. |
| **Bouncing** | Mark 1 or more Stress to hit that many targets in range of the attack. |
| **Brave** | -1 to Evasion; +3 to Severe damage threshold. |
| **Brutal** | When you roll the maximum value on a damage die, roll an additional damage die. |
| **Burning** | When you roll a 6 on a damage die, the target must mark a Stress. |
| **Concussive** | On a successful attack, you can spend a Hope to knock the target back to Far range. |
| **Cumbersome** | -1 to Finesse. |
| **Deflecting** | When attacked, mark an Armor Slot to gain a bonus to your Evasion equal to your available Armor Slots against the attack. |
| **Destructive** | -1 to Agility; on a successful attack, all adversaries within Very Close range must mark a Stress. |
| **Devastating** | Before you make an attack roll, you can mark a Stress to use a d20 as your damage die. |
| **Double Duty** | +1 to Armor Score; +1 to primary weapon damage within Melee range. |
| **Doubled Up** | When you make an attack with your primary weapon, you can deal damage to another target within Melee range. |
| **Dueling** | When there are no other creatures within Close range of the target, gain advantage on your attack roll against them. |
| **Grappling** | On a successful attack, you can spend a Hope to Restrain the target or pull them into Melee range with you. |
| **Greedy** | Spend a handful of credits to gain a +1 bonus to your Proficiency on a damage roll. |
| **Heavy** | -1 to Evasion. |
| **Hooked** | On a successful attack, you can pull the target into Melee range. |
| **Hot** | This weapon cuts through solid material. |
| **Invigorating** | On a successful attack, roll a d4. On a result of 4, clear a Stress. |
| **Lifestealing** | On a successful attack, roll a d6. On a result of 6, clear a Hit Point or clear a Stress. |
| **Locked On** | On a successful attack, your next attack against the same target with your primary weapon automatically succeeds. |
| **Long** | This weapon's attack targets all adversaries in a line within range. |
| **Massive** | -1 to Evasion; on a successful attack, roll an additional damage die and discard the lowest result. |
| **Otherworldly** | On a successful attack, you can deal kinetic or energy damage. |
| **Painful** | Each time you make a successful attack, you must mark a Stress. |
| **Paired** | Bonus to primary weapon damage to targets within Melee range. |
| **Parry** | When attacked, roll this weapon's damage dice. If any of the attacker's damage dice rolled the same value as your dice, the matching results are discarded from the attacker's damage dice before the damage you take is totaled. |
| **Persuasive** | Before you make a Presence Roll, you can mark a Stress to gain a +2 bonus to the result. |
| **Pompous** | You must have a Presence of 0 or lower to use this weapon. |
| **Powerful** | On a successful attack, roll an additional damage die and discard the lowest result. |
| **Protective** | Bonus to Armor Score. |
| **Quick** | When you make an attack, you can mark a Stress to target another creature within range. |
| **Reliable** | +1 to attack rolls. |
| **Reloading** | After you make an attack, roll a d6. On a result of 1, you must mark a Stress to reload this weapon before you can fire it again. |
| **Retractable** | The blade can be hidden in the hilt to avoid detection. |
| **Returning** | When this weapon is thrown within its range, it appears in your hand immediately after the attack. |
| **Scary** | On a successful attack, the target must mark a Stress. |
| **Serrated** | When you roll a 1 on a damage die, it deals 8 damage instead. |
| **Sharpwing** | Gain a bonus to your damage rolls equal to your Agility. |
| **Sheltering** | When you mark an Armor Slot, it reduces damage for you and all allies within Melee range of you who took the same damage. |
| **Startling** | Mark a Stress to crack the whip and force all adversaries within Melee range back to Close range. |
| **Timebending** | You can choose the target of your attack after making your attack roll. |
| **Versatile** | This weapon can also be used with alternate statistics (listed in the weapon description). |

---

chapters.title: 7. Consumables

sections.title: Medical Consumables
sections.content:
| Item Name | Effect | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Minor Combat Stim** | Clear 1d4 HP. | 1 Handful |
| **Minor Neural Stabilizer** | Clear 1d4 Stress. | 1 Handful |
| **Standard Combat Stim** | Clear 1d4+1 HP. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Standard Neural Stabilizer** | Clear 1d4+1 Stress. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Major Combat Stim** | Clear 1d4+2 HP. | 1 Bag |
| **Major Neural Stabilizer** | Clear 1d4+2 Stress. | 1 Bag |
| **Adrenaline Shot** | Mark a Stress and clear a HP. | 1 Handful |
| **Trauma Patch** | You can use this patch to spend any number of Hope and clear that many Armor Slots. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Xeno-Antidote** | Cures the effects of most common alien toxins and venoms. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Stasis Pod (Portable)** | If you have at least one Stasis Pod on you when you fall unconscious, you gain a +1 bonus to the roll you make to determine whether you gain a scar. | 1 Bag |

sections.title: Tactical Consumables
sections.content:
| Item Name | Effect | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Agility Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your next Agility Roll. | 1 Handful |
| **Strength Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your next Strength Roll. | 1 Handful |
| **Finesse Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your next Finesse Roll. | 1 Handful |
| **Instinct Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your next Instinct Roll. | 1 Handful |
| **Presence Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your next Presence Roll. | 1 Handful |
| **Knowledge Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your next Knowledge Roll. | 1 Handful |
| **Major Agility Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Agility until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Major Strength Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Strength until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Major Finesse Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Finesse until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Major Instinct Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Instinct until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Major Presence Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Presence until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Major Knowledge Enhancer** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Knowledge until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Smoke Grenade** | When an adversary attacks you, use this grenade and roll a number of d6 equal to your Agility. Add the highest result to your Evasion against the attack. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Flashbang Grenade** | You can activate this grenade to create a flash of bright light. All targets within Close range become Blinded until they mark HP. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Plasma Grenade** | You can make a Finesse Roll to throw this grenade at a group of adversaries within Far range. Targets you succeed against take 1d20 energy damage. | 1 Bag |
| **Improved Plasma Grenade** | As Plasma Grenade, but targets take 2d20 energy damage. | 3 Bags |
| **Major Plasma Grenade** | As Plasma Grenade, but targets take 4d20 energy damage. | 1 Chest |
| **Thermal Paste** | This paste eats away walls and other surfaces in bright flashes. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Weapon Coating (Corrosive)** | Apply to a weapon that deals kinetic damage to add a d6 to your next damage roll with that weapon. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Weapon Coating (Improved)** | Apply to a weapon that deals kinetic damage to add a d8 to your next damage roll with that weapon. | 1 Bag |
| **Weapon Coating (Major)** | Apply to a weapon that deals kinetic damage to add a d12 to your next damage roll with that weapon. | 3 Bags |

---

chapters.content: Consumables are single-use items that provide temporary buffs, healing, or tactical advantages. They are consumed upon use and must be replenished through purchase, crafting, or looting.

chapters.title: 8. Utility Gear

sections.title: Relics (One Per Character)
sections.content:
Relics are powerful artifacts that provide a permanent bonus to a character's Traits. A character can only carry **one** Relic at a time.

| Relic Name | Effect | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stride Relic** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Agility. | 3 Bags |
| **Bolster Relic** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Strength. | 3 Bags |
| **Control Relic** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Finesse. | 3 Bags |
| **Attune Relic** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Instinct. | 3 Bags |
| **Charm Relic** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Presence. | 3 Bags |
| **Enlighten Relic** | You gain a +1 bonus to your Knowledge. | 3 Bags |
| **Honing Relic** | You gain a +1 bonus to an Experience of your choice. | 3 Bags |

---

chapters.content:
Utility gear represents reusable tools and equipment that assist in exploration, infiltration, survival, and social encounters. Unlike consumables, utility gear is not destroyed on use unless otherwise noted.

| Item Name | Effect | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mag-Boots** | You can walk on the surface of starships in zero-G or climb up vertical metallic surfaces. | 3 Handfuls |
| **Holo-Disguise Emitter** | You can spend 4 Hope to use this emitter, altering your face enough to make you unrecognizable until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Personal Cloaking Device** | Once per long rest, you can activate this device to become Hidden until you move. While Hidden in this way, you remain unseen even if an adversary moves to where they would normally see you. | 3 Bags |
| **Breathing Apparatus** | You can use this mask to breathe in toxic environments or underwater for a number of minutes equal to your level. | 3 Handfuls |
| **Data-Spike** | By touching this spike to a terminal, you can perfectly copy the terminal's contents. Once used, this spike becomes useless. (Consumable) | 2 Handfuls |
| **Zero-G Thruster Pack** | You gain thrusters that allow you to fly in zero-G environments for a number of minutes equal to your level. | 1 Bag |
| **Sensor Jammer** | You can use this jammer to prevent anyone from tracking you by electronic or thermal means until your next rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Translation Earpiece** | This earpiece automatically translates most known alien languages in real-time. | 3 Handfuls |
| **Emergency Beacon** | When you activate this beacon, its signal can be detected by ships within a 1-system radius. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Portable Shield Generator** | Position this generator in a location of your choosing and activate it. All allies within Close range of it gain a +1 bonus to their Evasion. While activated, the generator can't be moved. Once deactivated, it can't be activated again until your next long rest. | 1 Bag |
| **Manacles (Mag-Lock)** | This pair of magnetic locking cuffs comes with a key. | 1 Handful |
| **Homing Compasses** | These two compasses point toward each other no matter how far apart they are. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Speaking Orbs** | This pair of orbs allows any creatures holding them to communicate with each other across any distance. | 1 Bag |
| **Woven Net** | You can make a Finesse Roll using this net to trap a small creature. A trapped target can break free with a successful Attack Roll (16). | 2 Handfuls |
| **Suspended Rod** | This flat rod is inscribed with runes. When you activate the rod, it is immediately suspended in place. Until deactivated, it can't move and doesn't abide by the rules of gravity. | 3 Handfuls |
| **Empty Chest** | This chest appears empty. When you speak a specific trigger word and open the chest, you can see the items stored within it. | 2 Handfuls |
| **Charging Quiver** | When you succeed on an attack with a projectile stored in this quiver, gain a bonus to the damage roll equal to your current tier. | 1 Bag |

sections.title: Carrying Capacity (Burden)
sections.content:
Characters in *Nexus 9* are limited by what they can reasonably carry into a firefight or diplomatic negotiation. This is represented by **Burden**.

**Weapons:** A character can carry a maximum of **two hands' worth** of weapons ready for immediate use (e.g., one Two-Handed weapon, or one One-Handed primary weapon and one One-Handed secondary weapon). They may carry one additional weapon stowed on their back or in a holster, which requires a Free Action to draw.

**Armor:** A character can only wear one suit of armor at a time.

**Consumables and Gear:** A character can carry up to **5 Consumables** and **3 pieces of Utility Gear** on their person without penalty. Carrying more requires a cargo bag, which imposes a -1 penalty to Agility rolls while worn.

sections.title: Acquiring Gear (The Credit Economy)
sections.content:
*Nexus 9* uses a simplified wealth system based on **Credits**. Instead of tracking individual coins, wealth is abstracted into tiers of purchasing power.

| Wealth Tier | Value | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Handful of Credits** | Pocket change | A meal, a cheap drink, a bribe to a dock worker, a single consumable |
| **Bag of Credits** | Moderate wealth | A night in a decent hotel, a Tier 1 weapon, a significant bribe, a shuttle ticket |
| **Chest of Credits** | Serious money | A Tier 3 weapon, a rare artifact, a small starship, a political favor |

**Standard Costs:**

| Item Category | Cost |
| :--- | :--- |
| Tier 1 Weapon or Armor | 1-5 Handfuls |
| Tier 2 Weapon or Armor | 1-2 Bags |
| Tier 3 Weapon or Armor | 5-10 Bags |
| Tier 4 Weapon or Armor | 1-2 Chests |
| Standard Consumable | 1 Handful |
| Utility Gear | 3 Handfuls |
| Relic | 3 Bags |
| Meals for a crew per night | 1 Handful |
| Standard quarters per night | 1 Handful |
| Luxury quarters per night | 1 Bag |
| Shuttle ride | 2 Handfuls |
| Specialized tools | 3 Handfuls |
| Fine clothing | 3 Handfuls |
| Luxury clothing | 1 Bag |

---

| Secondary Weapons | 7 | 10 | 8 | 10 | 35 |

| **Grand Total Items** | | | | | **199** |

chapters.title: Chapter 13: Starship Rules and Travel

sections.title: Ship Mechanics
sections.content:
*   **Hull Points (HP):** Represents the ship's physical integrity. When a ship reaches 0 HP, it is crippled, adrift, and vulnerable to boarding actions.
*   **System Stress:** Represents the strain on the ship's engines, shields, and life support. When a ship maxes out its System Stress, a critical system fails (e.g., weapons go offline, life support drops, or engines stall).
*   **Evasion:** The target number an attacker must roll to hit the ship, modified by the Pilot's Agility.

sections.title: Crew Roles and Actions
sections.content:
During starship combat, each player assumes a specific role on the ship. Each role has access to unique actions that contribute to the ship's survival and success.

*   **Helm (Pilot):** Controls the ship's movement. Can take the *Evasive Maneuvers* action (Agility roll) to increase the ship's Evasion for the round, or the *Pursuit* action to close the distance with a fleeing enemy.
*   **Tactical (Marine):** Controls the ship's weapons. Can take the *Fire Weapons* action (Finesse roll) to attack an enemy ship, or the *Target Lock* action to gain Advantage on the next attack.
*   **Engineering (Engineer):** Manages power and repairs. Can take the *Reroute Power* action (Knowledge roll) to clear 1 System Stress, or the *Emergency Repairs* action to restore 1d4 Hull Points.
*   **Sensors/Comms (Envoy/Mystic):** Manages information and electronic warfare. Can take the *Sensor Sweep* action (Instinct roll) to identify enemy weaknesses, or the *Jamming* action to impose Disadvantage on enemy attacks.

---

chapters.content: Starships in *Nexus 9* are treated as characters in their own right, with their own stats, modules, and damage tracks. Ship combat is designed to involve the entire crew, not just the pilot.

sections.title: Exploration Procedures
sections.content:
When the crew ventures into uncharted space or explores a derelict vessel, the GM uses Exploration Procedures to structure the tension.

*   **The Survey Roll:** Upon entering a new sector or boarding a derelict, the Sensor Operator (or any character with high Instinct) makes a Survey Roll. Success reveals the layout, life signs, and immediate hazards. Failure means the crew is ambushed or triggers a trap.
*   **The Hazard Die:** For every 10 minutes of in-game time spent exploring a dangerous environment, the GM rolls a d6. On a 1, a complication occurs (e.g., life support fails, a patrol spots them, or an environmental hazard activates).
*   **Salvage:** Exploring derelicts often yields valuable salvage. A successful Knowledge or Finesse roll allows a character to extract 1d10 x 100 Credits worth of usable parts or data.

sections.title: Ship Repair Rules
sections.content:
A crippled ship (0 HP) or a ship with maxed-out System Stress is a sitting duck. Repairs require time, resources, and skill.

*   **Emergency Repairs (In Combat):** The Engineer can take the *Emergency Repairs* action (Knowledge roll) to restore 1d4 Hull Points or clear 1 System Stress. This requires an Action Token and represents jury-rigging systems to keep the ship flying.
*   **Field Repairs (Out of Combat):** If the ship is safe but damaged, the crew can spend a short rest (1 hour) conducting field repairs. A successful Knowledge roll restores 1d8 Hull Points and clears all System Stress. This consumes 1 unit of Spare Parts (costing 500 Credits).
*   **Drydock Repairs:** Major structural damage requires a drydock facility (like those on Nexus 9). Drydock repairs cost 1,000 Credits per Hull Point restored and take 1 day per point.

---

chapters.title: Starship Combat
chapters.content:
## Running a Ship Battle

Starship combat in *Nexus 9* uses the same Hope and Fear spotlight system as personal combat, but each player acts through their **crew station** rather than as an individual combatant. The ship is a shared character — every crew member contributes to its survival.

This chapter provides the complete turn-by-turn procedure for running a starship engagement at the table.

---

## Before the Battle: Setup

When a starship encounter begins, the GM performs the following setup:

**1. Establish Range.** Determine the starting range between the crew's ship and the threat. Use the same range bands as personal combat (Melee through Very Far), but at ship scale:

| Ship Range Band | Description | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Melee** | Hulls are touching or docked | Boarding action; grappled by a tractor beam |
| **Very Close** | Within point-defense range | Dogfighting; close escort formation |
| **Close** | Standard weapons range | Broadside exchange; torpedo run |
| **Far** | Long-range sensors and weapons | Sniper shots; missile salvos; pursuit |
| **Very Far** | Edge of sensor range | Initial detection; long-range approach |

**2. Set the Fear Budget.** The GM places Fear tokens on the Action Tracker based on the encounter's intensity:

| Encounter Type | Starting Fear | Examples |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Minor Skirmish | 1-2 | A single pirate interceptor; a customs patrol |
| Standard Engagement | 3-5 | A Syndicate raiding party; a Terran patrol frigate |
| Major Battle | 6-8 | A capital ship engagement; an Eclipse Void-Shape attack |
| Climactic | 9-12 | The defense of Nexus 9; a fleet action |

**3. Assign Crew Stations.** Each player declares which station they are manning. A character can switch stations during combat by spending their action (they do nothing else that turn).

---

## Crew Stations and Actions

Each crew station has a set of actions available to it. When a player has the spotlight, they choose one action from their station and roll 2d12 + the relevant Trait.

### The Helm (Primary Trait: Agility)

The Helm controls the ship's movement and evasion.

| Action | Roll | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Evasive Maneuvers** | Agility vs. Difficulty | On success, the ship gains +2 Evasion until the Helm's next turn. On a critical success, +4. |
| **Pursue** | Agility vs. enemy Helm | On success, close the range by one band. On a critical success, close by two bands. |
| **Flee** | Agility vs. enemy Helm | On success, increase the range by one band. If you reach beyond Very Far, you escape the engagement. |
| **Ram** | Agility vs. enemy Evasion | On success, deal your ship's Hull Class in d6 damage to the enemy (e.g., a Courier deals 4d6). Both ships take half that damage. Must be at Melee range. |

### Tactical (Primary Trait: Instinct or Agility)

Tactical controls the ship's weapons systems.

| Action | Roll | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Fire Weapons** | Instinct vs. enemy Evasion | On success, roll the weapon's damage dice and apply to the enemy ship's damage thresholds. |
| **Target Lock** | Instinct vs. Difficulty 13 | On success, the next Fire Weapons action against the locked target gains Advantage. |
| **Suppressive Fire** | Instinct vs. Difficulty 13 | On success, the targeted enemy ship suffers Disadvantage on its next action. |
| **Torpedo/Missile Launch** | Agility vs. enemy Evasion | On success, deal heavy damage (listed on the weapon). Torpedoes are limited ammunition — track uses. |

### Engineering (Primary Trait: Knowledge)

Engineering manages power, shields, and emergency repairs.

| Action | Roll | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Reroute Power** | Knowledge vs. Difficulty 13 | On success, grant +2 to one other station's next roll (e.g., boost weapons, boost engines). |
| **Emergency Repair** | Knowledge vs. Difficulty 15 | On success, clear 1 System Stress from the ship. On a critical success, clear 2. |
| **Damage Control** | Knowledge vs. Difficulty 13 | On success, stabilize a Critical System Failure, preventing it from worsening for 1d4 rounds. |
| **Shield Boost** | Knowledge vs. Difficulty 15 | On success, the ship's damage thresholds increase by +3 until Engineering's next turn. |

### Sensors/Comms (Primary Trait: Knowledge or Presence)

Sensors/Comms handles scanning, electronic warfare, and communications.

| Action | Roll | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Sensor Sweep** | Knowledge vs. Difficulty 13 | On success, reveal one piece of tactical information about the enemy (weak point, remaining Hull Points, hidden ships, etc.). |
| **Electronic Warfare** | Knowledge vs. enemy Difficulty | On success, jam the enemy's sensors. Their next attack roll suffers Disadvantage. |
| **Distress Call** | Presence vs. Difficulty 15 | On success, reinforcements or assistance will arrive in 1d4 rounds. The GM determines who responds. |
| **Hail/Negotiate** | Presence vs. enemy Difficulty | On success, open communications. This may allow a diplomatic resolution, a bluff, or a surrender demand. |

### Quartermaster (Primary Trait: Presence or Knowledge)

The Quartermaster manages resources and crew morale during combat.

| Action | Roll | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Rally the Crew** | Presence vs. Difficulty 13 | On success, one crew member clears 1 Stress. On a critical success, all crew members clear 1 Stress. |
| **Distribute Stims** | No roll required | Spend 1 Hope. One crew member clears 2 HP. Uses one medical supply from the ship's stores. |
| **Jettison Cargo** | No roll required | Dump cargo to reduce mass. The ship gains +1 Evasion for the rest of the encounter, but the cargo is lost. |
| **Coordinate** | Presence vs. Difficulty 15 | On success, the next crew member to act gains Advantage on their roll. |

---

## The Ship Combat Round

Starship combat does not have fixed rounds. Like personal combat, it uses the **spotlight system**:

**1. The GM sets the scene** and describes the tactical situation. The first player to act is whoever makes narrative sense (usually the Sensors operator who detects the threat, or the Helm who is piloting when the attack begins).

**2. The active player chooses a station action** and rolls 2d12 + Trait.

**3. Determine Hope or Fear:**
- **Hope result:** The player gains 1 Hope and chooses which crew member acts next.
- **Fear result:** The GM gains 1 Fear and decides what happens next — another player may act, or the GM spends Fear to activate enemy ship actions.

**4. The GM spends Fear** to activate enemy ship actions:

| Enemy Ship Action | Fear Cost |
|:---|:---:|
| Fire weapons (one weapon system) | 1 Fear |
| Move one range band | 0 Fear (free with an attack) |
| Use a special system (listed on the ship's stat block) | 1-3 Fear |
| Launch fighters or boarding pods | 2 Fear |
| Activate reinforcements | 3-4 Fear |
| Escalation (the situation worsens dramatically) | 3-5 Fear |

**5. Repeat** until the encounter is resolved — one side is destroyed, disabled, surrenders, or escapes.

---

## Ship Damage

Ship damage works identically to personal damage, using the ship's Frame Class thresholds:

| Damage Total vs. Ship Thresholds | Hull Points Lost | System Stress Marked |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Below Minor | 0 | 0 |
| Meets/Exceeds Minor | 1 | 0 |
| Meets/Exceeds Major | 2 | 1 |
| Meets/Exceeds Severe | 3 | 2 |

When a ship's **System Stress** is maxed out, the GM rolls on the **Critical System Failure Table** (see Book Four).

When a ship's **Hull Points** reach 0, the ship is **disabled** — it is dead in space, venting atmosphere, and the crew must abandon ship or attempt emergency repairs (Engineering roll, Difficulty 20).

---

## Boarding Actions

When two ships are at Melee range (hulls touching), either side may attempt a boarding action. Boarding transitions the encounter from ship-scale to personal-scale combat:

**1. The attacking ship's Helm** must succeed on an Agility roll (Difficulty 13) to lock docking clamps or breach the hull.

**2. Once locked**, the boarding party (some or all of the crew) enters the enemy ship. Combat switches to personal-scale rules.

**3. The remaining crew** on the player's ship continue to operate their stations at ship scale, defending against enemy fighters, maintaining systems, or providing fire support.

**4. The boarding party** fights through the enemy ship using personal combat rules. Their objective might be to reach the bridge, disable the engines, rescue prisoners, or plant explosives.

---

## Pursuit and Escape

If one side attempts to flee, the engagement becomes a **pursuit**:

**1.** The fleeing ship's Helm rolls Agility vs. the pursuing ship's Helm roll each spotlight turn.

**2.** On success, the fleeing ship increases range by one band. On failure, the range stays the same (or closes if the pursuer rolled a critical success).

**3.** If the fleeing ship reaches **beyond Very Far range**, it escapes. If it has a functioning hyperspace drive, it can attempt an emergency jump (Knowledge roll, Difficulty 15). Success means immediate escape; failure means the drive misfires and the ship drops out at a random, potentially dangerous location.

---

## Quick Reference: Ship Combat Checklist

| Step | Action |
|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Establish starting range and Fear budget |
| 2 | Players declare crew stations |
| 3 | First player takes the spotlight |
| 4 | Player chooses a station action and rolls 2d12 + Trait |
| 5 | Resolve Hope/Fear — spotlight passes |
| 6 | GM spends Fear for enemy actions |
| 7 | Repeat until encounter resolves |
| 8 | If ships reach Melee, boarding is possible |
| 9 | If a ship flees beyond Very Far, it escapes |
